./reviews/coalgebraic-transcendentals-review.md:35:3.  **(Completeness) Haskell/Lean Artifacts:** The conclusion mentions accompanying Haskell and Lean 4 files. While this is a review of the paper itself, it is worth noting that the claim of having a Lean artifact formalising the coinduction principle as "classical, set-level" is slightly at odds with the paper's emphasis on working within constructive, cubical HoTT. This is a very minor point of internal consistency between the paper's narrative and its claimed supplementary materials. A more HoTT-aligned formalisation would presumably use a cubical library. This does not detract from the paper's quality but is a small point of friction.
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:6:<li><p><strong>ETCS (Lawvere 1964)</strong> replaces the universe of sets-with-membership by a <em>category</em> of sets-with-functions, axiomatised structurally as a well-pointed topos with a natural numbers object (NNO) and a choice operator;</p></li>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:40:<td style="text-align: left;">NNO existence</td>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:107:<p><a href="#sec:etcs" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:etcs">2</a> states the eight-axiom presentation of ETCS, proves the elementary consequences (NNO, products, exponentials), and recalls McLarty’s bi-interpretation theorem with Bounded Zermelo + Replacement. <a href="#sec:izf" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:izf">3</a> presents IZF, contrasts it with CZF and ZFC, and discusses the realisability and topos-valued models that connect IZF to HoTT. <a href="#sec:folds" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:folds">4</a> develops Makkai’s signature theory, defines FOLDS-equivalence, and proves Makkai’s Invariance Theorem (every FOLDS-formula is invariant under FOLDS-equivalence). <a href="#sec:hott" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:hott">5</a> reframes HoTT as the unifying framework: which structural principles become theorems, which need univalence. <a href="#sec:models" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:models">6</a> surveys models: ETCS in toposes, FOLDS in fibrations, HoTT in <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes. <a href="#sec:boundary" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:boundary">7</a> formally analyses the univalence boundary. <a href="#sec:open" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:open">8</a> states three open problems. <a href="#sec:conclusion" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:conclusion">12</a> concludes.</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:126:<p><em>Remark 4</em>.  A1+A2+A3 alone make <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{S}\)</span> an <em>elementary topos</em> in the sense of Lawvere–Tierney. Adding A4 gives a <em>topos with NNO</em>. Adding A5–A8 specialises to “topos behaving like classical sets”. Equivalently, an ETCS model is a well-pointed topos with NNO and choice. Two-valuedness (A7) follows from well-pointedness plus non-degeneracy when AC is present.</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:146:<p><em>Sketch.</em> Given a model of ETCS, define a class of <em>trees</em> as well-founded extensional quotients of pointed graphs internalised by the NNO and powerset; this gives a model of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{BZ}+\mathbf{R}\)</span>. Conversely, given a model <span class="math inline">\(V\)</span> of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{BZ}+\mathbf{R}\)</span>, define <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{S}_V\)</span> to be the category of sets and functions inside <span class="math inline">\(V\)</span>; one verifies A1–A8 directly. Composition produces models that are equivalent in the appropriate 2-categorical sense. Full proof in McLarty <span class="citation" data-cites="McLarty2004"></span>; we return to it in <a href="#sec:models" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="sec:models">6</a>. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:154:<p>We illustrate how arithmetic is developed structurally in ETCS using only the universal property of the NNO. Given the NNO <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},0,s)\)</span>, define addition <span class="math inline">\(+:\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\)</span> as follows.</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:166:<p><em>Proof.</em> Apply the NNO universal property to the pointed endomorphism <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N}^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{N}} : 1\to \mathbb{N}^{\mathbb{N}},
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:174:<p>Removing A6 (choice) yields what is sometimes called <em>ETCS-AC</em> or <em>Topos+NNO+WP</em> (well-pointed topos with NNO). This system is consistent with both EM and <span class="math inline">\(\neg\)</span>EM in non-trivial models. McLarty’s bi-interpretation theorem extends to: ETCS-AC is bi-interpretable with <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{BZ}+\mathbf{R}\)</span>-AC. The constructive variant of ETCS (replace A5 well-pointedness by Kock’s notion of subterminal-pointedness, drop A6 and A7) is bi-interpretable with appropriate fragments of IZF. We will see in <a href="#sec:boundary" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="sec:boundary">7</a> that AC is on the <em>straddling</em> side of the univalence boundary.</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:327:<li><p><strong>NNO</strong> is a theorem in HoTT — the inductive type <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span> is built into the type theory and contractibility of the type of NNO structures is provable (Paper V Theorem 4.4 of our series).</p></li>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:347:<p><strong>Theorem 33</strong> (Sets in HoTT form a model of ETCS-AC).  <em>Within HoTT, define <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{Set}\)</span> to be the type of <span class="math inline">\(0\)</span>-truncated types (sets in the sense of HoTT §3.1): <span class="math display">\[\mathrm{Set} := \Sigma_{X:\mathcal{U}}\,\mathrm{isSet}(X).\]</span> Then <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{Set}\)</span>, with the obvious morphism structure (functions between sets) and identity and composition, is an elementary topos with NNO. Adding propositional resizing and the (provably independent) axioms EM and AC produces a model of ETCS.</em></p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:350:<p><em>Sketch.</em> <strong>Finite limits</strong>: Sigma-types and product types are sets when their components are sets; equalisers are <span class="math inline">\(\Sigma_{x:A} (f(x) = g(x))\)</span>, also a set. <strong>Cartesian closure</strong>: function types <span class="math inline">\(A\to B\)</span> are sets when <span class="math inline">\(B\)</span> is a set (by FE). <strong>Subobject classifier</strong>: <span class="math inline">\(\Omega := \mathrm{hProp}\)</span>, the type of mere propositions; truth is the standard map. <strong>NNO</strong>: the inductive type <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>. <strong>Well-pointedness</strong>: function extensionality. <strong>AC</strong>: axiom (independent in HoTT). <strong>Two-valuedness</strong>: requires EM (axiom). <strong>Non-degeneracy</strong>: <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{0}\not\simeq\mathbf{1}\)</span> holds in any non-trivial model. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:369:<p>A model of ETCS is a well-pointed elementary topos with NNO and AC. The category <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{Set}\)</span> in ZFC is the prototype. Other models include:</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:373:<li><p><span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{FinSet}\)</span>: finite sets satisfy A1–A6, fail A4 (no NNO), so not ETCS.</p></li>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:377:<p>Friedman–Aczel construction: in any cocomplete elementary topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> with NNO and a Mahlo-style universe object, the internal <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{V}\)</span>-structure (the “cumulative hierarchy” inside <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>) gives a model of IZF.</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:480:<p><strong>Theorem 42</strong> (Near-side: NNO existence is near).  <em>The existence of an NNO in HoTT (as the inductive type <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>) does not require UA.</em></p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:511:<td style="text-align: left;"><strong>NNO</strong></td>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:573:<li><p>NNO existence (theorem of MLTT, no UA needed).</p></li>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:581:<p>A proof would mirror Paper V Theorem 4.4 (contractibility of the NNO type) but at the level of the entire categorical universe. Tools needed: SIP for <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-toposes, internal <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-Yoneda (Riehl–Shulman directed type theory).</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:584:<p><strong>Conjecture 47</strong>.  <em>There is a Cubical Agda library implementing FOLDS signatures, FOLDS-equivalence, and <span class="math inline">\(n\)</span>-level FOLDS (Szumiło 2019), and proving <a href="#thm:up" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:up">31</a> formally for <span class="math inline">\(n\le 2\)</span> (equivalence of FOLDS-equivalence and HoTT-identification at <span class="math inline">\(n=0,1,2\)</span>).</em></p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:586:<p>Partial work exists in the agda-categories library and in Tsementzis’s formalisation of the SIP. Full FOLDS in Cubical Agda would close the gap between Makkai 1995 and ANST 2019 syntactically.</p>
./docs/papers/etcs-izf-folds.html:726:<p>The univalence boundary precisely separates: the principles that hold in MLTT alone (the near side: NNO, induction, basic structural reasoning), and the principles requiring univalence (the far side: SIP, FOLDS-equivalence-as-identity, the full equivalence principle). Mapping out this boundary is, we suggest, one of the more illuminating exercises in 2026 foundations.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:2:<p>The natural numbers object (NNO) is among the most basic universal constructions of categorical logic. Lawvere’s 1964 axiomatisation of the elementary theory of the category of sets (ETCS) treats the NNO as an irreducible primitive on equal footing with the terminal object, the subobject classifier, and exponentials. In any cartesian closed category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span> with a terminal object <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{1}\)</span>, an NNO is a triple <span class="math inline">\((N, 0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N, s\colon N\to N)\)</span> such that for every <span class="math inline">\((X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)\)</span> there exists a unique morphism <span class="math inline">\(h\colon N\to X\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(h\circ 0 = x_{0}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(h\circ s = f\circ h\)</span>. Two NNOs are related by a unique isomorphism; the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial. In <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{Set}\)</span> the NNO is the von Neumann set <span class="math inline">\(\omega = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}\)</span> with successor; up to canonical isomorphism, “the natural numbers” is a property, not a choice of structure.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:3:<p>The HoTT version is finer. Working in the univalent universe <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{U}\)</span>, define <span class="math display">\[\mathrm{NNO}\;\coloneqq\; \Sigma_{X:\mathcal{U}}\,\Sigma_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigma_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),\]</span> where <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)\)</span> denotes the proposition that <span class="math inline">\((X,x_{0},s)\)</span> is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem 2.7 of Paper III<a href="#fn1" class="footnote-ref" id="fnref1" role="doc-noteref"><sup>1</sup></a> (which we recapitulate in §<a href="#sec:background" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:background">2</a> as <a href="#thm:contr-1cat" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-1cat">7</a>) and Theorem 4.4 of Paper V state that <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> is contractible—a <span class="math inline">\((-2)\)</span>-type. The centre of contraction is <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ},\star)\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span> is the inductive type of natural numbers and <span class="math inline">\(\star\)</span> is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than “unique up to unique iso”: it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a <span class="math inline">\(2\)</span>-path, ad infinitum.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:4:<p>By Shulman’s theorem <span class="citation" data-cites="Shulman2019"></span>, every Grothendieck <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> there is an internal type of NNO structures in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-categorical statement: the space <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> of NNO structures, formed in the <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>, is a contractible Kan complex.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:5:<p>The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO available in the literature: Lurie’s parametrised NNO <span class="citation" data-cites="LurieHTT LurieSAG"></span>, formulated for presentable <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh’s NNO <span class="citation" data-cites="Rasekh2021"></span>, which lives in any elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos and is constructed from the loop space of <span class="math inline">\(S^{1}\)</span>. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:8:<li><p>We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (§<a href="#sec:background" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:background">2</a>, <a href="#thm:contr-1cat" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-1cat">7</a>), recall Lambek’s theorem at the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoids.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:9:<li><p>We give a precise account of Lurie’s parametrised NNO (§<a href="#sec:lurie" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:lurie">3</a>, <a href="#def:lurie-nno" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:lurie-nno">12</a>, <a href="#thm:lurie-nno-equiv" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:lurie-nno-equiv">20</a>) and prove its equivalence with the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-categorical NNO when <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> is the underlying <span class="math inline">\((2,1)\)</span>-category of a <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-topos.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:10:<li><p>We formalise the higher coherence problem (§<a href="#sec:higher-coh" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:higher-coh">4</a>, <a href="#prop:tower" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="prop:tower">21</a>) as the requirement that, for each <span class="math inline">\(n\geq 0\)</span>, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to <span class="math inline">\(n\)</span>-homotopy.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:11:<li><p>We prove our main theorem (§<a href="#sec:main" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:main">5</a>, <a href="#thm:contractibility-infty" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contractibility-infty">23</a>): in any <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> that admits a HoTT model, the space <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> is contractible.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:12:<li><p>We compare with Rasekh’s circle-construction (§<a href="#sec:rasekh" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:rasekh">6</a>) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:16:<p><a href="#sec:background" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:background">2</a> reviews the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-categorical NNO, Lambek’s theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper III/V. <a href="#sec:lurie" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:lurie">3</a> introduces Lurie’s parametrised NNO and Rasekh’s elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO. <a href="#sec:higher-coh" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:higher-coh">4</a> formalises the higher-coherence tower. <a href="#sec:main" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:main">5</a> contains the main contractibility theorem. <a href="#sec:rasekh" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:rasekh">6</a> relates the construction to the loop-space-of-<span class="math inline">\(S^{1}\)</span> approach. <a href="#sec:stt" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:stt">7</a> discusses synthetic approaches. <a href="#sec:open" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:open">10</a> closes with open problems.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:19:<h1 data-number="2" id="sec:background"><span class="header-section-number">2</span> Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility</h1>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:21:<h2 data-number="2.1" id="pointed-endomorphisms-and-1-nno"><span class="header-section-number">2.1</span> Pointed endomorphisms and <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-NNO</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:26:<p><strong>Definition 2</strong> (NNO).  A natural numbers object in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span> is an initial object of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{C})\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:28:<p>Equivalently, viewing the endofunctor <span class="math inline">\(F\colon \mathcal{C}\to \mathcal{C}\)</span> given by <span class="math inline">\(FX = \mathbf{1}+X\)</span> (when coproducts exist), an NNO is an initial <span class="math inline">\(F\)</span>-algebra: a pair <span class="math inline">\((N,[0,s]\colon \mathbf{1}+N\to N)\)</span> initial in <span class="math inline">\(F\text{-}\mathbf{Alg}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:48:<p><strong>Theorem 6</strong> (Universal property of NNO).  <em>Let <span class="math inline">\((N,0,s)\)</span> be an NNO in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span>. For every <span class="math inline">\((X,x_{0},f)\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{C})\)</span> there exists a unique <span class="math inline">\(h\colon N\to X\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(h\circ 0 = x_{0}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(h\circ s = f\circ h\)</span>.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:50:<h2 data-number="2.2" id="sec:rigidity-1cat"><span class="header-section-number">2.2</span> 1-Categorical contractibility (rigidity of NNOs)</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:52:<p>In <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{Set}\)</span>, the NNO is <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},0,\mathrm{succ})\)</span>. By <a href="#lem:rigid" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="lem:rigid">3</a> the groupoid of NNOs in <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{Set}\)</span> has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a <span class="math inline">\((-2)\)</span>-truncated space.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:54:<p><strong>Theorem 7</strong> (1-categorical contractibility of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span>).  <em>In any category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span> with terminal object, the groupoid <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{C})\)</span> of NNO structures is either empty or contractible (i.e. a <span class="math inline">\((-2)\)</span>-type). Equivalently, the moduli space of NNOs in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span> is a (mere) proposition: existence implies essential uniqueness, with no nontrivial automorphisms or higher cells (which are absent at the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-categorical level).</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:57:<p><em>Proof.</em> <a href="#lem:rigid" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="lem:rigid">3</a> provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{1}\)</span>, hence contractible. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:62:<p><strong>Definition 8</strong> (Type of NNOs in HoTT).  <span class="math display">\[\mathrm{NNO}\;\coloneqq\; \Sigma_{X:\mathcal{U}}\,\Sigma_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigma_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),\]</span> where <span class="math display">\[\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s) \;\coloneqq\; \Pi_{(Y,y_{0},f):\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{U})}\,\mathsf{isContr}\!\bigl(\Sigma_{h:X\to Y}(h\,x_{0}=y_{0})\times\Pi_{x:X}(h(s\,x)=f(h\,x))\bigr).\]</span></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:66:<p><strong>Theorem 9</strong> (Contractibility of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> in HoTT <span class="citation" data-cites="PaperIII PaperV"></span>).  <em>The type <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> is contractible. The centre of contraction is <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ},\star)\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span> is the inductive type of natural numbers and <span class="math inline">\(\star\)</span> is the canonical witness of initiality given by the standard recursor <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{rec}_{\mathbb{N}}\)</span> together with its computation rules.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:69:<p><em>Proof.</em> We must show <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}(\mathrm{NNO})\)</span>, i.e. produce a centre <span class="math inline">\(c\in\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> and a continuous family of paths from <span class="math inline">\(c\)</span> to every point of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:71:<p><em>Contraction onto the centre.</em> Take any <span class="math inline">\((X,x_{0},s,\iota)\in\mathrm{NNO}\)</span>. We must produce a path <span class="math display">\[p\colon (\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ},\star) \;=\; (X,x_{0},s,\iota) \quad\text{in}\quad \mathrm{NNO}.\]</span> Apply <span class="math inline">\(\iota\)</span> to the pointed system <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ})\)</span>: it yields a contractible type whose centre is a unique map <span class="math inline">\(u\colon X\to \mathbb{N}\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(u\,x_{0}=\mathsf{zero}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(u\circ s\sim \mathsf{succ}\circ u\)</span>. Apply <span class="math inline">\(\star\)</span> to <span class="math inline">\((X,x_{0},s)\)</span>: a unique map <span class="math inline">\(v\colon \mathbb{N}\to X\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(v\,\mathsf{zero}=x_{0}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(v\circ \mathsf{succ}\sim s\circ v\)</span>. The composites <span class="math inline">\(u\circ v\colon \mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{N}}\)</span> both satisfy the universal-property equations for <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ},\star)\)</span>; uniqueness inside the contractible type of recursors forces <span class="math inline">\(u\circ v=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{N}}\)</span> via <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{ind}_{\mathbb{N}}\)</span>, and likewise <span class="math inline">\(v\circ u=\mathrm{id}_{X}\)</span> via <span class="math inline">\(\iota\)</span>. Thus <span class="math inline">\(u\colon X\simeq\mathbb{N}\)</span> is an equivalence (with quasi-inverse <span class="math inline">\(v\)</span>). Univalence (<span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{ua}\)</span>) converts the equivalence into a path <span class="math inline">\(p_{1}\colon X = \mathbb{N}\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{U}\)</span>. Transporting <span class="math inline">\((x_{0},s,\iota)\)</span> along <span class="math inline">\(p_{1}\)</span> produces <span class="math inline">\((\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ},\star&#39;)\)</span> for some witness <span class="math inline">\(\star&#39;\colon \mathsf{isInitial}(\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ})\)</span>. Since <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isInitial}\)</span> is a proposition, <span class="math inline">\(\star&#39;=\star\)</span>. Pairing yields the required path <span class="math inline">\(p\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:75:<p><em>Remark 10</em> (The infinite tower).  The proof of <a href="#thm:contr-hott" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-hott">9</a> bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}(\mathrm{NNO})\)</span>. Path induction (the <span class="math inline">\(J\)</span>-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, <span class="math inline">\(2\)</span>-paths, <span class="math inline">\(3\)</span>-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}(\mathrm{NNO})\)</span>, we automatically get <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}(\Omega^{n}\mathrm{NNO})\)</span> for every <span class="math inline">\(n\)</span>, by general HoTT lemmas.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:78:<p><em>Remark 11</em> (Why this is stronger).  <a href="#thm:contr-1cat" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="thm:contr-1cat">7</a> says “unique iso between any two NNOs”. <a href="#thm:contr-hott" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="thm:contr-hott">9</a> says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of <span class="math inline">\(2\)</span>-paths is contractible, and so on. In a <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}\)</span> is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:80:<h1 data-number="3" id="sec:lurie"><span class="header-section-number">3</span> The <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical NNO</h1>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:82:<h2 data-number="3.1" id="luries-parametrised-nno"><span class="header-section-number">3.1</span> Lurie’s parametrised NNO</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:84:<p><strong>Definition 12</strong> (<span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO, Lurie).  Let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> be a presentable <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos with terminal object <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{1}\)</span>. An NNO is an object <span class="math inline">\(N\in\mathcal{E}\)</span> together with morphisms <span class="math inline">\(0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(s\colon N\to N\)</span> such that for every diagram <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{1}\xrightarrow{x_{0}}X\xrightarrow{f}X\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>, the mapping space <span class="math display">\[\mathrm{Map}_{\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})}\bigl((N,0,s),(X,x_{0},f)\bigr)\]</span> is contractible.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:88:<p><em>Remark 13</em>.  Equivalently, an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO is an initial object in <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})\)</span>, where “initial” is interpreted in the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical sense: the mapping space to every other object is contractible. Lurie phrases the definition via a small-colimit-preserving recursion functor; the equivalence is straightforward.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:91:<p><em>Example 14</em>.  When <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}=\mathcal{S}\)</span>, the NNO is the constant functor at <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>. Its existence and contractibility of the mapping space is the homotopy-coherent version of the Set-theoretic recursion principle.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:94:<p><em>Example 15</em>.  For <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})\)</span> the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category of presheaves of spaces on a small <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span>, the NNO is the constant presheaf at <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>, <span class="math inline">\(c\mapsto \mathbb{N}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:97:<p><em>Example 16</em>.  For <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{Sh}(X)\)</span> the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos of sheaves on a topological space <span class="math inline">\(X\)</span>, the NNO is the constant sheaf <span class="math inline">\(\underline{\mathbb{N}}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:101:<p><strong>Theorem 17</strong> (Existence in presentable case).  <em>Every presentable <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos has an NNO.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:106:<h2 data-number="3.3" id="elementary-infty1-toposes-and-rasekhs-nno"><span class="header-section-number">3.3</span> Elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes and Rasekh’s NNO</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:107:<p>A more delicate question is whether every <em>elementary</em> <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh <span class="citation" data-cites="Rasekh2018ETop"></span>. Elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes are axiomatised by:</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:117:<p><strong>Theorem 18</strong> (Rasekh <span class="citation" data-cites="Rasekh2021"></span>).  <em>Every elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos has an NNO. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:120:<p><em>Sketch of Rasekh’s argument.</em> The circle <span class="math inline">\(S^{1}\)</span> exists in any elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space <span class="math inline">\(\Omega S^{1}\)</span> has a <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{Z}\)</span>-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:123:<p><em>Remark 19</em>.  Rasekh’s proof is striking because it derives a discrete, <span class="math inline">\(0\)</span>-truncated, NNO from a manifestly <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-truncated object, <span class="math inline">\(S^{1}\)</span>, via genuinely <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-categorical machinery. There is no <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-categorical analogue: <span class="math inline">\(\Omega S^{1}\)</span> in the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-topos of sets is a singleton.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:125:<h2 data-number="3.4" id="equivalence-with-the-1-nno-when-mathcale-is-a-1-topos"><span class="header-section-number">3.4</span> Equivalence with the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-NNO when <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> is a <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-topos</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:127:<p><strong>Theorem 20</strong> (Comparison).  <em>Let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> be a <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-topos with NNO <span class="math inline">\((N,0,s)\)</span>, and let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}\)</span> be its embedding as a <span class="math inline">\((2,1)\)</span>-category, viewed as an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category. Then <span class="math inline">\((N,0,s)\)</span> is an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO of <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}\)</span> in the sense of <a href="#def:lurie-nno" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:lurie-nno">12</a>.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:133:<p>Why is the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO “more delicate”? The naive answer is: in a <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-category, “unique map” is a single condition. In an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category, “unique map” must be promoted to “unique up to coherent homotopy”. Concretely, given two NNOs <span class="math inline">\(N_{1}, N_{2}\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>, we need a comparison map <span class="math inline">\(u\colon N_{1}\to N_{2}\)</span> together with the homotopies filling the squares <span class="math display">\[\begin{tikzcd}[column sep=large, row sep=large]
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:143:<p><strong>Proposition 21</strong> (Initiality absorbs the entire coherence tower).  <em>The initiality of an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO already encodes, in a single property, the entire infinite tower of coherence data sketched above. Concretely, for any two <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNOs <span class="math inline">\((N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\((N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})\)</span> in an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>, the mapping space <span class="math display">\[M \;\coloneqq\; \mathrm{Map}_{\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})}\bigl((N_{1},0_{1},s_{1}),(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})\bigr)\]</span> is contractible.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:146:<p><em>Proof.</em> By definition of <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO, both <span class="math inline">\((N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\((N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})\)</span> are initial objects of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})\)</span>. Initial objects in any <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category have contractible mapping spaces to every object, in particular to each other. Contractibility of <span class="math inline">\(M\)</span> is equivalent to existence of a <span class="math inline">\(0\)</span>-cell (the comparison map <span class="math inline">\(u\)</span> and the homotopies <span class="math inline">\(\alpha,\beta\)</span>), uniqueness up to a contractible space of <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-cells (the next-level coherences), uniqueness of those up to a contractible space of <span class="math inline">\(2\)</span>-cells, and so on inductively at every level <span class="math inline">\(n\geq 0\)</span>. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:155:<h2 data-number="4.1" id="truncation-level-of-mathrmnnomathcale"><span class="header-section-number">4.1</span> Truncation level of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span></h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:156:<p>Define <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> as the full subspace of the <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoid <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})^{\simeq}\)</span> (the maximal sub-<span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoid of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})\)</span>) on objects that are initial.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:158:<p><strong>Theorem 23</strong> (Contractibility of the <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-NNO space).  <em>For any <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> in which an NNO exists, the space <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> is contractible.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:163:<h1 data-number="5" id="sec:main"><span class="header-section-number">5</span> The main theorem: contractibility for <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNOs</h1>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:167:<p><strong>Theorem 24</strong> (Main theorem).  <em>Let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> be an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos that admits a model of HoTT + univalence (e.g. any Grothendieck <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos, by <span class="citation" data-cites="Shulman2019"></span>). Then:</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:169:<li><p><em>There exists an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> (<a href="#def:lurie-nno" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:lurie-nno">12</a>).</em></p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:170:<li><p><em>The space <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> is contractible (a <span class="math inline">\((-2)\)</span>-type).</em></p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:171:<li><p><em>Any two <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNOs in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> are connected by an essentially unique equivalence.</em></p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:172:<li><p><em>The automorphism <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-group of an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO is contractible.</em></p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:176:<p><em>Proof.</em> By Shulman’s theorem, <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> admits a model of HoTT in which <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{U}\)</span> is interpreted as the object classifier and the type <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> of <a href="#def:nno-type" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:nno-type">8</a> is interpreted as an internal object of <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> (more precisely, as the underlying object of an <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoid object of <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>, classified by <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{U}\)</span>). By <a href="#thm:contr-hott" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-hott">9</a>, this internal type is contractible (i.e. the proposition <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}(\mathrm{NNO})\)</span> has a global element).</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:177:<p><em>Externalisation.</em> The functor <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\to \mathcal{S}\)</span>, <span class="math inline">\(X\mapsto \mathrm{Map}_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)\)</span>, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{1}\)</span> is the unit of the model) takes the internal type <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> to the external <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoid <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span>. Internal contractibility of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> in the HoTT model is the global element of <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isContr}(\mathrm{NNO})\)</span>, which under externalisation becomes a global point of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{Map}_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\mathsf{isContr}\,\mathrm{NNO})\simeq\mathsf{isContr}(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))\)</span>, witnessing that <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> is contractible in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{S}\)</span>. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see <span class="citation" data-cites="KapulkinLumsdaine"></span>.)</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:191:<h2 data-number="5.3" id="identification-with-rasekhs-nno"><span class="header-section-number">5.3</span> Identification with Rasekh’s NNO</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:193:<p><strong>Theorem 26</strong> (Rasekh <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> Lurie internally).  <em>Let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> be an elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of <span class="math inline">\(S^{1}\)</span> coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO in the sense of <a href="#def:lurie-nno" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:lurie-nno">12</a> (when both are defined).</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:196:<p><em>Proof.</em> By part (2) of <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a>, <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh’s NNO (<a href="#thm:rasekh" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:rasekh">18</a>) and any Lurie-style NNO (<a href="#def:lurie-nno" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:lurie-nno">12</a>) are points of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span>. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:198:<h1 data-number="6" id="sec:rasekh"><span class="header-section-number">6</span> Rasekh’s circle-construction of the NNO</h1>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:227:<p><strong>Proposition 31</strong> (Object classifier suffices).  <em>In an elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos with object classifier <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{U}\)</span>, the NNO can be constructed without separate appeal to power objects.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:241:<h2 data-number="7.2" id="an-nno-in-stt"><span class="header-section-number">7.2</span> An NNO in STT</h2>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:242:<p>In STT, one can define the type <span class="math display">\[\mathrm{NNO}_{\mathrm{dir}} \;\coloneqq\; \Sigma_{X}\,\Sigma_{x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X}\,\Sigma_{s\colon X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}(X,x_{0},s),\]</span> where <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}\)</span> uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}_{\mathrm{dir}}\)</span> should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT—a property partially proved by Gratzer–Weinberger–Buchholtz <span class="citation" data-cites="GWB2024"></span> for the universe of discrete types.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:244:<p><em>Remark 32</em> (Open).  A complete contractibility result for <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}_{\mathrm{dir}}\)</span> in the universe of discrete types of <span class="citation" data-cites="GWB2024"></span> would give a synthetic proof of <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a>, internal to STT. We are not aware of a published proof.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:249:<p><em>Remark 33</em>.  In cubical type theory, the contractibility of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> (<a href="#thm:contr-hott" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-hott">9</a>) becomes a constructive theorem: the centre of contraction <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ},\star)\)</span> can be computed, and the contraction map sends each <span class="math inline">\((X,x_{0},s,\iota)\)</span> to a concrete cube witnessing the path. This is the basis for a Cubical Agda formalisation; we leave such a formalisation to future work.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:252:<p><em>Remark 34</em> (Cubical NNO).  The constructive content of Lambek’s theorem (<a href="#lem:lambek" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="lem:lambek">4</a>) is also more transparent in cubical type theory: the inverse <span class="math inline">\(h\colon \mathbb{N}\to\mathbf{1}+\mathbb{N}\)</span> of the structure map is computed by case analysis on <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{zero}\)</span> vs <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{succ}\,n\)</span>, with the round-trip equations <span class="math inline">\(\iota\circ h=\mathrm{id}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(h\circ\iota=\mathrm{id}\)</span> holding judgmentally.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:260:<p>For the NNO, all three internal languages give equivalent answers when restricted to discrete types, but only the first two have published contractibility theorems.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:262:<p>A useful recasting of the NNO is as the free pointed dynamical system on the empty set.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:264:<p><strong>Theorem 35</strong> (NNO as left adjoint to a forgetful).  <em>The forgetful functor <span class="math inline">\(U\colon \mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathbf{Set})\to \mathbf{Set}\)</span>, <span class="math inline">\((X,x_{0},f)\mapsto X\)</span>, has a left adjoint <span class="math inline">\(F\colon \mathbf{Set}\to\mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathbf{Set})\)</span>. Moreover, <span class="math inline">\(F(\emptyset)=(\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ})\)</span>, the NNO of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{Set}\)</span>.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:277:<p>The same construction applies in any <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> with finite coproducts and pullbacks: <span class="math inline">\(F\dashv U\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(U\colon \mathbf{PtEndo}(\mathcal{E})\to \mathcal{E}\)</span> is the underlying-object functor. The image of the empty (initial) object under <span class="math inline">\(F\)</span> is the NNO of <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:279:<p><em>Remark 37</em>.  This is the cleanest abstract characterisation: the NNO is the value of the free-pointed-dynamical-system functor at the initial object. <a href="#thm:contr-hott" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-hott">9</a> then says that this functor is well-defined up to a contractible space of choices.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:281:<h1 data-number="9" id="sec:lurie-param"><span class="header-section-number">9</span> Lurie’s parametrised NNO in detail</h1>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:284:<p><strong>Definition 38</strong> (Parametrised NNO <span class="citation" data-cites="LurieHTT"></span>).  Let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> be an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos and let <span class="math inline">\(S\in\mathcal{E}\)</span>. A relative NNO over <span class="math inline">\(S\)</span> is an object <span class="math inline">\(N_{S}\to S\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}/S\)</span>, equipped with <span class="math inline">\(0_{S}\colon S\to N_{S}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(s_{S}\colon N_{S}\to N_{S}\)</span> over <span class="math inline">\(S\)</span>, such that <span class="math inline">\((N_{S},0_{S},s_{S})\)</span> is the NNO of the slice <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}/S\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:288:<p><strong>Theorem 39</strong> (Parametrisation is contractibly functorial).  <em>The assignment <span class="math inline">\(S\mapsto \mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}/S)\)</span> extends to a functor <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{op}}\to \mathcal{S}\)</span>, valued in contractible spaces. Equivalently, the parametrised NNO is, up to a contractible space of choices, functorial in the base.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:291:<p><em>Sketch.</em> Functoriality is a consequence of pullback functoriality of slices: a morphism <span class="math inline">\(f\colon S&#39;\to S\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> induces <span class="math inline">\(f^{*}\colon \mathcal{E}/S\to \mathcal{E}/S&#39;\)</span>, which preserves the NNO universal property. Contractibility of each <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}/S)\)</span> is <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a> applied to the slice. The two together give a homotopy-coherent functor; the precise claim is that the space of such functors is itself contractible. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:294:<p><em>Remark 40</em> (Globalness).  Theorem <a href="#thm:param-functorial" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:param-functorial">39</a> is the precise statement of “the NNO is uniquely determined globally, not just locally.” In a <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-topos this would be a single global iso between any two parametrised NNOs; in an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos it is a contractible space of comparison data, which is the right <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-categorical strengthening.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:297:<p>In stable <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categories (or more generally in <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes with stable structure), the NNO can be seen as the truncation of a more refined object: the sphere spectrum <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{S}\)</span>, whose underlying space is <span class="math inline">\(\Omega^{\infty}\mathbb{S}\simeq\prod_{n\geq 0}K(\pi_{n}\mathbb{S},n)\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:299:<p><em>Remark 41</em>.  The <span class="math inline">\(0\)</span>-truncation of the connective sphere spectrum <span class="math inline">\(\tau_{\leq 0}\mathbb{S}\)</span> is the discrete <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{Z}\)</span>, whose non-negative part is the NNO. This relates the NNO to the homotopy theory of spectra and chromatic homotopy theory.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:303:<li><p><strong>Internal-to-external transfer.</strong> Make the externalisation step in the proof of <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a> fully precise. This includes coherent strictification of the internal type <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> to an external <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoid.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:305:<li><p><strong>Synthetic NNO contractibility in STT.</strong> Prove that <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}_{\mathrm{dir}}\)</span> is contractible in simplicial type theory, using only Rezk-completeness of the universe of discrete types.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:307:<li><p><strong>Lurie’s parametrised NNO.</strong> Compare the parametrised version (over a base <span class="math inline">\(S\in\mathcal{E}\)</span>) with the absolute version studied here. Show that the parametrisation is functorial up to contractible choice.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:332:<p>In a locally cartesian closed <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category, the NNO satisfies dependent induction.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:334:<p><strong>Theorem 46</strong> (Dependent induction from NNO).  <em>Let <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> be locally cartesian closed with NNO <span class="math inline">\((N,0,s)\)</span>. For any morphism <span class="math inline">\(P\colon N\to \mathcal{U}\)</span>, with <span class="math inline">\(p_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to P(0)\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(p_{s}\colon \prod_{n:N}\,P(n)\to P(\mathsf{succ}\,n)\)</span>, there is a unique section <span class="math inline">\(\widetilde{p}\colon \prod_{n:N}\,P(n)\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(\widetilde{p}(0)=p_{0}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(\widetilde{p}(\mathsf{succ}\,n)=p_{s}(\widetilde{p}(n))\)</span>.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:337:<p><em>Sketch.</em> Apply iteration in the slice <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}/N\)</span>. The total space <span class="math inline">\(\Sigma_{n:N}\,P(n)\)</span> becomes a pointed dynamical system over <span class="math inline">\(N\)</span>, and the NNO universal property in the slice gives the section. Locally cartesian closure ensures the slice has the requisite structure. By contractibility (<a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a>), the section is unique up to a contractible space of choices. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:342:<p>The <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{S}\)</span> of <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoids (“spaces”) is the terminal <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos. By <a href="#thm:exist-pres" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:exist-pres">17</a> it has an NNO; concretely, this is the discrete groupoid <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span> viewed as a <span class="math inline">\(0\)</span>-truncated space.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:344:<p><em>Example 47</em> (NNO in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{S}\)</span>).  The space <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{S})\)</span> has as objects pairs <span class="math inline">\((X,\rho)\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(X\in\mathcal{S}\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(\rho\)</span> is initial-data identifying <span class="math inline">\(X\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>. By <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a>(2), <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{S})\)</span> is contractible. Concretely, every object can be connected to <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathrm{id})\)</span> by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice path, and the higher-dimensional cells of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{S})\)</span> are all contractible.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:347:<p>For a small <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-category <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span>, the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})\coloneqq\mathrm{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathcal{S})\)</span> is presentable, hence by <a href="#thm:exist-pres" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:exist-pres">17</a> has an NNO.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:349:<p><strong>Proposition 48</strong>.  <em>The NNO of <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})\)</span> is the constant functor <span class="math inline">\(c\mapsto\mathbb{N}\in\mathcal{S}\)</span>, with structure maps inherited pointwise. Its space of NNO structures is contractible.</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:352:<p><em>Proof.</em> Since the constant functor <span class="math inline">\(\Delta\colon\mathcal{S}\to\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})\)</span> preserves limits (it is right adjoint to the colimit-evaluation), it sends the NNO <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\in\mathcal{S}\)</span> to a pointed dynamical system in <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})\)</span>, denoted <span class="math inline">\(\Delta(\mathbb{N})\)</span>. The recursion principle for <span class="math inline">\(\Delta(\mathbb{N})\)</span> is verified pointwise. Contractibility follows from <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a> applied to <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})\)</span>. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:355:<p><em>Example 49</em> (Simplicial spaces).  For <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}=\Delta^{\mathrm{op}}\)</span>, <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{PSh}(\Delta^{\mathrm{op}})\)</span> is the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos of simplicial spaces; the NNO is the bisimplicial set whose every level is the discrete space <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:359:<p><em>Example 50</em> (Sheaf <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos).  For a topological space <span class="math inline">\(T\)</span> (or, more generally, a Grothendieck site <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{C}\)</span>), the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{Sh}(T)\)</span> has as NNO the constant sheaf <span class="math inline">\(\underline{\mathbb{N}}\)</span>, which assigns to each open <span class="math inline">\(U\subseteq T\)</span> the discrete space of locally constant <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>-valued functions <span class="math inline">\(U\to\mathbb{N}\)</span>. Equivalently, <span class="math inline">\(\underline{\mathbb{N}}=\mathrm{colim}_{n\geq 0}\,\mathbf{1}_{T}^{\sqcup n}\)</span>. Its NNO-space is contractible by <a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a>.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:362:<p>When <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> is a cohesive <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="citation" data-cites="SchreiberShulman"></span>, the NNO sits in the discrete sub-<span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{disc}(\mathcal{S})\hookrightarrow\mathcal{E}\)</span> and remains contractible there. The cohesive structure adds geometric information (smooth, differential, supersmooth) but does not affect the discrete arithmetic; this is the modular composition pattern in action.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:364:<p>We pause to relate the universal-property NNO to Peano’s axioms, as a sanity check that all the abstract machinery agrees with the classical picture.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:366:<p><strong>Theorem 51</strong> (Equivalence NNO <span class="math inline">\(\Leftrightarrow\)</span> Peano in <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{Set}\)</span>).  <em>A triple <span class="math inline">\((N,0\colon\mathbf{1}\to N,s\colon N\to N)\)</span> in <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{Set}\)</span> is an NNO iff it satisfies the Peano axioms:</em></p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:376:<p><em>Sketch.</em> (<span class="math inline">\(\Rightarrow\)</span>) Suppose <span class="math inline">\((N,0,s)\)</span> is an NNO. (P1)–(P2) are immediate. (P3): the recursor for <span class="math inline">\((\{T,F\},T,\neg)\)</span> produces <span class="math inline">\(h\colon N\to\{T,F\}\)</span> with <span class="math inline">\(h(0)=T\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(h(s\,n)=\neg h(n)\)</span>; injectivity of <span class="math inline">\(\{T,F\}\)</span>-valued recursion forces <span class="math inline">\(s(n)\neq 0\)</span>. (P4) is more subtle: a clever choice of pointed dynamical system (with carrier <span class="math inline">\(1+N\)</span>) yields a left-inverse to <span class="math inline">\(s\)</span>, hence injectivity. (P5) is the universal property restricted to subsets, characteristic-functioned via <span class="math inline">\(\{T,F\}\)</span>. (<span class="math inline">\(\Leftarrow\)</span>) Conversely, Peano’s axioms define a unique-up-to-iso initial pointed dynamical system; the iteration construction yields the recursor. ◻</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:379:<p><em>Remark 52</em>.  In an arbitrary <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-topos, “injectivity” must be expressed via monomorphisms, “not equal” via disjointness in <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{1}\amalg N\)</span>, and “induction over subsets” via the subobject classifier. The proof goes through with these modifications. In an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos, induction must additionally be replaced by induction over <span class="math inline">\((-1)\)</span>-truncated maps; the contractibility theorem ensures this still gives an NNO.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:391:<p>The contractibility theorem solves, at the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf’s problem <span class="citation" data-cites="Benacerraf1965"></span>: “which set is the natural numbers?” becomes “which <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-groupoid is the natural numbers?” The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same—and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond “the type of NNOs is contractible.”</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:393:<p>In the modular framework adopted by the broader research programme, the NNO sits at the bottom of a tower:</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:395:<li><p><strong>NNO</strong> (this paper): provides arithmetic.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:401:<p>The contractibility of NNO at the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-level ensures that everything built on top of NNO inherits a unique, canonical foundation. This is the modular composition principle: each layer is uniquely determined by the previous one, up to contractible choice.</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:406:<li><p>The connection to Rasekh’s circle-construction is via contractibility: we know the two NNOs agree, but we do not give an explicit comparison map.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:412:<li><p>the <span class="math inline">\(1\)</span>-categorical NNO and its contractibility (<a href="#thm:contr-1cat" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-1cat">7</a>);</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:413:<li><p>Lurie’s parametrised <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO (<a href="#def:lurie-nno" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:lurie-nno">12</a>) and its existence for presentable <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> (<a href="#thm:exist-pres" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:exist-pres">17</a>);</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:414:<li><p>Rasekh’s circle-construction of an NNO in any elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos (<a href="#thm:rasekh" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:rasekh">18</a>);</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:415:<li><p>the main contractibility theorem (<a href="#thm:main" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:main">24</a>) showing <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> is contractible whenever HoTT models in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>;</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:422:<p>The companion source tree at <code>src/infinity-nno/</code> contains a Haskell encoding of the NNO universal property. The relevant types and functions are:</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:424:<li><p><code>NNO.PtEndo a</code> – a record type with fields <code>ptBase :: a</code> and <code>ptStep :: a -&gt; a</code>, representing a pointed dynamical system on a Haskell type <code>a</code>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:425:<li><p><code>NNO.rec :: PtEndo a -&gt; Integer -&gt; a</code> – the recursor witnessing the universal property of <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{N},\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ})\)</span>. By construction <code>rec pe 0 == ptBase pe</code> and <code>rec pe (n+1) == ptStep pe (rec pe n)</code> for <span class="math inline">\(n\geq 0\)</span>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:426:<li><p><code>NNO.lambekIso :: Integer -&gt; Bool</code> – a property check that the structure map <span class="math inline">\([\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ}]\colon\mathbf{1}+\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\)</span> is invertible (Lambek’s theorem in operational form).</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:427:<li><p><code>Properties.runChecks :: Bool</code> – a battery of property checks combining <code>prop_rec_zero</code>, <code>prop_rec_step</code>, and <code>prop_lambek</code>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:428:<li><p><code>Proofs.canonicalIsoIsId</code> and <code>Proofs.uniqueAutoIsId</code> – operational counterparts of <a href="#lem:rigid" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="lem:rigid">3</a> and <a href="#thm:contr-1cat" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="thm:contr-1cat">7</a>, demonstrating that the canonical iso between two NNOs is the identity and that the automorphism group is trivial.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:430:<p>The <code>Main.hs</code> module exhibits the universal property on concrete examples including factorial via primitive recursion (<a href="#prop:prim-rec" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="prop:prim-rec">43</a>).</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:432:<p>The companion Lean file <code>lean/infinity-nno/NNO.lean</code> contains a self-contained mechanisation of the universal property and Lambek’s theorem, suitable for use with Mathlib4. Highlights:</p>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:434:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.PtEndo</code> – a structure on a type <span class="math inline">\(\alpha\)</span> with fields <code>base : </code><span class="math inline">\(\alpha\)</span> and <code>step : </code><span class="math inline">\(\alpha\to\alpha\)</span>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:435:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.rec</code> – the recursor, defined by pattern matching on <code>Nat</code>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:436:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.recExists</code> – existence half of the universal property: <span class="math inline">\(\exists\,h\colon\mathbb{N}\to\alpha\)</span> satisfying both equations.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:437:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.recUnique</code> – uniqueness, by induction on <code>Nat</code>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:438:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.recUniversal</code> – combined statement: the recursor is the unique such function.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:439:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.lambek</code> – the structure map <span class="math inline">\([\mathsf{zero},\mathsf{succ}]\colon\mathrm{Unit}\oplus\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\)</span> is bijective.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:440:<li><p><code>InfinityNNO.aut_is_id</code> – any successor-preserving automorphism of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span> fixing <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{zero}\)</span> is the identity.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:449:<li><p><span class="math inline">\((N,0,s)\)</span> – an NNO: object, basepoint, successor.</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:451:<li><p><span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}\)</span> – in HoTT, the type of NNO structures (<a href="#def:nno-type" data-reference-type="ref+label" data-reference="def:nno-type">8</a>).</p></li>
./docs/papers/infinity-nno.html:452:<li><p><span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})\)</span> – the external space of NNO structures in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span>.</p></li>
./reviews/etcs-izf-folds-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:11:1. `src/etcs-izf-folds/Properties.hs:2-7` claims testing invariance under FOLDS-equivalence, but the properties only test bijective relabeling of one fixed model and preserve counts (`src/etcs-izf-folds/Properties.hs:29-37`, `106-124`). Counts of objects/arrows/identities are not atomic FOLDS predicates and are not invariant under categorical/FOLDS equivalence. A one-object category is equivalent to a two-object category with isomorphic objects, but `|O|`, `|A|`, and identity count differ.
./reviews/etcs-izf-folds-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:14:2. The sample category's composition table is not a valid category table. `src/etcs-izf-folds/Properties.hs:34` gives five triples for the category with `id0`, `id1`, and `f : 0 -> 1`; under either conventional tuple order, at least one of `(0,2,2)` or `(2,1,2)` is ill-typed, and the table should have four identity/unit composites.
./reviews/etcs-izf-folds-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:24:Concrete fix: add an aggregate `ETCS` class requiring finite limits, exponentials, subobject classifier, NNO, well-pointedness, choice, two-valuedness, and non-degeneracy; add `Mono`/`Epi` witnesses and law-checking functions for the universal properties.
./reviews/etcs-izf-folds-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:26:6. `proof_invarianceUnderRelabel` accepts arbitrary models and only compares predicate outputs (`src/etcs-izf-folds/Proofs.hs:103-110`). The demo passes two manually chosen models with equal counts (`src/etcs-izf-folds/Proofs.hs:137-144`), so it is not proving or checking relabeling/FOLDS equivalence.
./reviews/etcs-izf-folds-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:29:7. QuickCheck coverage is too shallow and failures are not actionable. The generator only permutes `modelM` (`src/etcs-izf-folds/Properties.hs:100-104`), never generates arbitrary finite categories, invalid structures, or equivalence spans; `reportTest` discards failure details and keeps the process successful (`src/etcs-izf-folds/Properties.hs:153-155`). I ran `./src/etcs-izf-folds/test`; it exits 0, but the passing tests are mostly tautological count-preservation checks.
./reviews/etcs-izf-folds-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:30:Concrete fix: add generators for law-checked finite categories and verified isomorphisms/equivalences, add negative tests for malformed spans, and make the runner return `IO Bool` or call `exitFailure` on any QuickCheck failure.
./docs/papers/coalgebraic-transcendentals.html:3:<p>The synthesis paper of our series <span class="citation" data-cites="synthesis"></span> identified six mutually equivalent presentations of the natural numbers (NNO, Peano, primitive recursion, set-theoretic encoding, Yoneda representable, HoTT inductive type). For the real numbers the analogue narrative produces two presentations rather than six, but each is itself a different <em>universal property</em>:</p>
./docs/papers/coalgebraic-transcendentals.html:399:<p>An accompanying executable framework is provided in Haskell: the stream functor, unfold/corecursion, the carry-bisimulation, and the spigot algorithms for <span class="math inline">\(\pi\)</span> (Leibniz, Machin) and <span class="math inline">\(e\)</span> (factorial series), together with QuickCheck properties for stream operations and approximation round-trips. The Lean 4 companion file (using Mathlib’s <code>Stream’</code> API) formalises the basic coalgebra structure, the corecursive <code>unfold</code>, and a (classical, set-level) coinduction principle; it does <em>not</em> include a formal proof of the productivity of the Sale or BBP transitions, which is left as <a href="#prob:lean-productivity" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="prob:lean-productivity">54</a>. The Lean component should be read as a partial certification of the type-theoretic infrastructure, not as a complete formalisation of the main theorems. The full formalisation effort is substantial: the main open problem (<a href="#conj:zeta" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="conj:zeta">50</a>) is the construction of a HoTT-native coalgebraic Riemann zeta function with <span class="math inline">\(\zeta(s) = 0\)</span> as a bisimulation-closed predicate; this remains far from solved.</p>
./docs/papers/coalgebraic-transcendentals.html:440:<p>Long, M. (2026). <em>Universal Properties of Real Numbers and Transcendentals</em>. Paper III, YonedaAI HoTT Foundations Series.</p>
./docs/papers/coalgebraic-transcendentals.html:460:<p>Coquand, T. (1994). Infinite objects in type theory. <em>Types for Proofs and Programs</em>, Springer LNCS.</p>
