./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:97:    57	\newcommand{\NNO}{\mathrm{NNO}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:141:   101	      {GrokRxiv:2026.05.infinity-nno\quad
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:149:   109	Contractibility, $\infty$-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:164:   124	The natural numbers object (NNO) of an elementary topos is, classically, an object equipped with a global element $0$ and an endomorphism $s$ such that the resulting pointed dynamical system is initial. In a $1$-topos any two NNOs are connected by a unique isomorphism, and the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial: NNO structures form a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated $\infty$-groupoid. In homotopy type theory (HoTT), Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III (and the equivalent Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V of the present series) refines this rigidity: the type
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:166:   126	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:168:   128	is contractible. By Shulman's interpretation theorem, every $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT~+~univalence, so the corresponding statement transports to an internal contractibility result for the type of NNO structures inside $\mathcal{E}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:170:   130	This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:181:   141	The natural numbers object (NNO) is among the most basic universal constructions of categorical logic. Lawvere's 1964 axiomatisation of the elementary theory of the category of sets (ETCS) treats the NNO as an irreducible primitive on equal footing with the terminal object, the subobject classifier, and exponentials. In any cartesian closed category $\mathcal{C}$ with a terminal object $\mathbf{1}$, an NNO is a triple $(N, 0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N, s\colon N\to N)$ such that for every $(X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)$ there exists a unique morphism $h\colon N\to X$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$. Two NNOs are related by a unique isomorphism; the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial. In $\Set$ the NNO is the von Neumann set $\omega = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ with successor; up to canonical isomorphism, ``the natural numbers'' is a property, not a choice of structure.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:185:   145	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:187:   147	where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:189:   149	By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:191:   151	The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:196:   156	  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:197:   157	  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:198:   158	  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:199:   159	  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:200:   160	  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:206:   166	\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:212:   172	\section{Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility}\label{sec:background}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:216:   176	\subsection{Pointed endomorphisms and \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:222:   182	\begin{definition}[NNO]\label{def:nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:226:   186	Equivalently, viewing the endofunctor $F\colon \mathcal{C}\to \mathcal{C}$ given by $FX = \mathbf{1}+X$ (when coproducts exist), an NNO is an initial $F$-algebra: a pair $(N,[0,s]\colon \mathbf{1}+N\to N)$ initial in $F\text{-}\mathbf{Alg}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:263:   223	\begin{theorem}[Universal property of NNO]\label{thm:up}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:264:   224	Let $(N,0,s)$ be an NNO in $\mathcal{C}$. For every $(X,x_{0},f)$ in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{C})$ there exists a unique $h\colon N\to X$ in $\mathcal{C}$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:267:   227	\subsection{1-Categorical contractibility (rigidity of NNOs)}\label{sec:rigidity-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:271:   231	In $\Set$, the NNO is $(\N,0,\mathrm{succ})$. By \cref{lem:rigid} the groupoid of NNOs in $\Set$ has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated space.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:273:   233	\begin{theorem}[1-categorical contractibility of $\NNO$]\label{thm:contr-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:274:   234	In any category $\mathcal{C}$ with terminal object, the groupoid $\NNO(\mathcal{C})$ of NNO structures is either empty or contractible (i.e.\ a $(-2)$-type). Equivalently, the moduli space of NNOs in $\mathcal{C}$ is a (mere) proposition: existence implies essential uniqueness, with no nontrivial automorphisms or higher cells (which are absent at the $1$-categorical level).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:278:   238	\Cref{lem:rigid} provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid $\mathbf{1}$, hence contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:285:   245	\begin{definition}[Type of NNOs in HoTT]\label{def:nno-type}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:287:   247	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:297:   257	\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of $\NNO$ in HoTT~\cite{PaperIII,PaperV}]\label{thm:contr-hott}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:298:   258	The type $\NNO$ is contractible. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical witness of initiality given by the standard recursor $\rec_{\N}$ together with its computation rules.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:302:   262	We must show $\isContr(\NNO)$, i.e.\ produce a centre $c\in\NNO$ and a continuous family of paths from $c$ to every point of $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:310:   270	\emph{Contraction onto the centre.} Take any $(X,x_{0},s,\iota)\in\NNO$. We must produce a path
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:312:   272	p\colon (\N,\zero,\suc,\star) \;=\; (X,x_{0},s,\iota) \quad\text{in}\quad \NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:314:   274	Apply $\iota$ to the pointed system $(\N,\zero,\suc)$: it yields a contractible type whose centre is a unique map $u\colon X\to \N$ with $u\,x_{0}=\zero$ and $u\circ s\sim \suc\circ u$. Apply $\star$ to $(X,x_{0},s)$: a unique map $v\colon \N\to X$ with $v\,\zero=x_{0}$ and $v\circ \suc\sim s\circ v$. The composites $u\circ v\colon \N\to\N$ and $\id_{\N}$ both satisfy the universal-property equations for $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$; uniqueness inside the contractible type of recursors forces $u\circ v=\id_{\N}$ via $\ind_{\N}$, and likewise $v\circ u=\id_{X}$ via $\iota$. Thus $u\colon X\eqv\N$ is an equivalence (with quasi-inverse $v$). Univalence ($\ua$) converts the equivalence into a path $p_{1}\colon X = \N$ in $\U$. Transporting $(x_{0},s,\iota)$ along $p_{1}$ produces $(\zero,\suc,\star')$ for some witness $\star'\colon \mathsf{isInitial}(\N,\zero,\suc)$. Since $\mathsf{isInitial}$ is a proposition, $\star'=\star$. Pairing yields the required path $p$ in $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:320:   280	The proof of \cref{thm:contr-hott} bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact $\isContr(\NNO)$. Path induction (the $J$-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, $2$-paths, $3$-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have $\isContr(\NNO)$, we automatically get $\isContr(\Omega^{n}\NNO)$ for every $n$, by general HoTT lemmas.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:324:   284	\Cref{thm:contr-1cat} says ``unique iso between any two NNOs''. \Cref{thm:contr-hott} says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of $2$-paths is contractible, and so on. In a $1$-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an $\infty$-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because $\isContr$ is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:327:   287	\section{The \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-categorical NNO}\label{sec:lurie}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:331:   291	\subsection{Lurie's parametrised NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:333:   293	\begin{definition}[$(\infty,1)$-NNO, Lurie]\label{def:lurie-nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:334:   294	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos with terminal object $\mathbf{1}$. An NNO is an object $N\in\mathcal{E}$ together with morphisms $0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N$ and $s\colon N\to N$ such that for every diagram $\mathbf{1}\xrightarrow{x_{0}}X\xrightarrow{f}X$ in $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:348:   308	Equivalently, an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is an initial object in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$, where ``initial'' is interpreted in the $(\infty,1)$-categorical sense: the mapping space to every other object is contractible. Lurie phrases the definition via a small-colimit-preserving recursion functor; the equivalence is straightforward.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:352:   312	When $\mathcal{E}=\Spc$, the NNO is the constant functor at $\N$. Its existence and contractibility of the mapping space is the homotopy-coherent version of the Set-theoretic recursion principle.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:356:   316	For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})$ the $(\infty,1)$-category of presheaves of spaces on a small $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{C}$, the NNO is the constant presheaf at $\N$, $c\mapsto \N$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:360:   320	For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ the $(\infty,1)$-topos of sheaves on a topological space $X$, the NNO is the constant sheaf $\underline{\N}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:366:   326	Every presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:377:   337	\subsection{Elementary \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-toposes and Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:379:   339	A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:390:   350	Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO\@. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:394:   354	The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO\@. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:398:   358	Rasekh's proof is striking because it derives a discrete, $0$-truncated, NNO from a manifestly $1$-truncated object, $\Sone$, via genuinely $\infty$-categorical machinery. There is no $1$-categorical analogue: $\Omega\Sone$ in the $1$-topos of sets is a singleton.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:401:   361	\subsection{Equivalence with the \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO when \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{E}$}{E} is a \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-topos}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:404:   364	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:417:   377	Why is the $(\infty,1)$-NNO ``more delicate''? The naive answer is: in a $1$-category, ``unique map'' is a single condition. In an $(\infty,1)$-category, ``unique map'' must be promoted to ``unique up to coherent homotopy''. Concretely, given two NNOs $N_{1}, N_{2}$ in $\mathcal{E}$, we need a comparison map $u\colon N_{1}\to N_{2}$ together with the homotopies filling the squares
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:432:   392	The initiality of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO already encodes, in a single property, the entire infinite tower of coherence data sketched above. Concretely, for any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ in an $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:440:   400	By definition of $(\infty,1)$-NNO, both $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ are initial objects of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$. Initial objects in any $(\infty,1)$-category have contractible mapping spaces to every object, in particular to each other. Contractibility of $M$ is equivalent to existence of a $0$-cell (the comparison map $u$ and the homotopies $\alpha,\beta$), uniqueness up to a contractible space of $1$-cells (the next-level coherences), uniqueness of those up to a contractible space of $2$-cells, and so on inductively at every level $n\geq 0$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:451:   411	\subsection{Truncation level of \texorpdfstring{$\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$}{NNO(E)}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:453:   413	Define $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ as the full subspace of the $\infty$-groupoid $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})^{\eqv}$ (the maximal sub-$\infty$-groupoid of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$) on objects that are initial.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:455:   415	\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of the $\infty$-NNO space]\label{thm:contractibility-infty}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:456:   416	For any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ in which an NNO exists, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:463:   423	\section{The main theorem: contractibility for \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-NNOs}\label{sec:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:469:   429	\begin{theorem}[Main theorem]\label{thm:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:472:   432	  \item There exists an $(\infty,1)$-NNO in $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{def:lurie-nno}).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:473:   433	  \item The space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible (a $(-2)$-type).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:474:   434	  \item Any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs in $\mathcal{E}$ are connected by an essentially unique equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:475:   435	  \item The automorphism $\infty$-group of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:480:   440	By Shulman's theorem, $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT in which $\U$ is interpreted as the object classifier and the type $\NNO$ of \cref{def:nno-type} is interpreted as an internal object of $\mathcal{E}$ (more precisely, as the underlying object of an $\infty$-groupoid object of $\mathcal{E}$, classified by $\U$). By \cref{thm:contr-hott}, this internal type is contractible (i.e.\ the proposition $\isContr(\NNO)$ has a global element).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:482:   442	\emph{Externalisation.} The functor $\mathcal{E}\to \Spc$, $X\mapsto \Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)$, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Internal contractibility of $\NNO$ in the HoTT model is the global element of $\isContr(\NNO)$, which under externalisation becomes a global point of $\Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\isContr\,\NNO)\eqv\isContr(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))$, witnessing that $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible in $\Spc$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:500:   460	\subsection{Identification with Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:503:   463	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of $\Sone$ coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based $(\infty,1)$-NNO in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno} (when both are defined).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:507:   467	By part~(2) of \cref{thm:main}, $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh's NNO (\cref{thm:rasekh}) and any Lurie-style NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) are points of $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:510:   470	\section{Rasekh's circle-construction of the NNO}\label{sec:rasekh}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:543:   503	In an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos with object classifier $\U$, the NNO can be constructed without separate appeal to power objects.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:554:   514	\subsection{An NNO in STT}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:558:   518	\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}} \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X}\,\Sigmatype_{s\colon X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:560:   520	where $\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}$ uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:563:   523	A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of~\cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT\@. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:574:   534	For the NNO, all three internal languages give equivalent answers when restricted to discrete types, but only the first two have published contractibility theorems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:580:   540	  Make the externalisation step in the proof of \cref{thm:main} fully precise. This includes coherent strictification of the internal type $\NNO$ to an external $\infty$-groupoid.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:583:   543	  \item \textbf{Synthetic NNO contractibility in STT.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:584:   544	  Prove that $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ is contractible in simplicial type theory, using only Rezk-completeness of the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:587:   547	  \item \textbf{Lurie's parametrised NNO.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:599:   559	The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?'' The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:603:   563	In the modular framework adopted by the broader research programme, the NNO sits at the bottom of a tower:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:605:   565	  \item \textbf{NNO} (this paper): provides arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:611:   571	The contractibility of NNO at the $(\infty,1)$-level ensures that everything built on top of NNO inherits a unique, canonical foundation. This is the modular composition principle: each layer is uniquely determined by the previous one, up to contractible choice.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:618:   578	  \item The connection to Rasekh's circle-construction is via contractibility: we know the two NNOs agree, but we do not give an explicit comparison map.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:627:   587	  \item the $1$-categorical NNO and its contractibility (\cref{thm:contr-1cat});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:628:   588	  \item Lurie's parametrised $(\infty,1)$-NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) and its existence for presentable $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{thm:exist-pres});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:629:   589	  \item Rasekh's circle-construction of an NNO in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos (\cref{thm:rasekh});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:630:   590	  \item the main contractibility theorem (\cref{thm:main}) showing $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever HoTT models in $\mathcal{E}$;

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1270:   287	\section{The \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-categorical NNO}\label{sec:lurie}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1274:   291	\subsection{Lurie's parametrised NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1276:   293	\begin{definition}[$(\infty,1)$-NNO, Lurie]\label{def:lurie-nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1277:   294	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos with terminal object $\mathbf{1}$. An NNO is an object $N\in\mathcal{E}$ together with morphisms $0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N$ and $s\colon N\to N$ such that for every diagram $\mathbf{1}\xrightarrow{x_{0}}X\xrightarrow{f}X$ in $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1291:   308	Equivalently, an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is an initial object in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$, where ``initial'' is interpreted in the $(\infty,1)$-categorical sense: the mapping space to every other object is contractible. Lurie phrases the definition via a small-colimit-preserving recursion functor; the equivalence is straightforward.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1295:   312	When $\mathcal{E}=\Spc$, the NNO is the constant functor at $\N$. Its existence and contractibility of the mapping space is the homotopy-coherent version of the Set-theoretic recursion principle.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1299:   316	For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})$ the $(\infty,1)$-category of presheaves of spaces on a small $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{C}$, the NNO is the constant presheaf at $\N$, $c\mapsto \N$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1303:   320	For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ the $(\infty,1)$-topos of sheaves on a topological space $X$, the NNO is the constant sheaf $\underline{\N}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1309:   326	Every presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1320:   337	\subsection{Elementary \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-toposes and Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1322:   339	A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1333:   350	Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO\@. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1337:   354	The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO\@. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1341:   358	Rasekh's proof is striking because it derives a discrete, $0$-truncated, NNO from a manifestly $1$-truncated object, $\Sone$, via genuinely $\infty$-categorical machinery. There is no $1$-categorical analogue: $\Omega\Sone$ in the $1$-topos of sets is a singleton.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1344:   361	\subsection{Equivalence with the \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO when \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{E}$}{E} is a \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-topos}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1347:   364	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1360:   377	Why is the $(\infty,1)$-NNO ``more delicate''? The naive answer is: in a $1$-category, ``unique map'' is a single condition. In an $(\infty,1)$-category, ``unique map'' must be promoted to ``unique up to coherent homotopy''. Concretely, given two NNOs $N_{1}, N_{2}$ in $\mathcal{E}$, we need a comparison map $u\colon N_{1}\to N_{2}$ together with the homotopies filling the squares
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1375:   392	The initiality of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO already encodes, in a single property, the entire infinite tower of coherence data sketched above. Concretely, for any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ in an $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1383:   400	By definition of $(\infty,1)$-NNO, both $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ are initial objects of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$. Initial objects in any $(\infty,1)$-category have contractible mapping spaces to every object, in particular to each other. Contractibility of $M$ is equivalent to existence of a $0$-cell (the comparison map $u$ and the homotopies $\alpha,\beta$), uniqueness up to a contractible space of $1$-cells (the next-level coherences), uniqueness of those up to a contractible space of $2$-cells, and so on inductively at every level $n\geq 0$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1394:   411	\subsection{Truncation level of \texorpdfstring{$\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$}{NNO(E)}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1396:   413	Define $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ as the full subspace of the $\infty$-groupoid $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})^{\eqv}$ (the maximal sub-$\infty$-groupoid of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$) on objects that are initial.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1446:147:where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1447:156:  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1448:157:  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1449:158:  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1450:159:  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1451:231:In $\Set$, the NNO is $(\N,0,\mathrm{succ})$. By \cref{lem:rigid} the groupoid of NNOs in $\Set$ has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated space.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1452:280:The proof of \cref{thm:contr-hott} bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact $\isContr(\NNO)$. Path induction (the $J$-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, $2$-paths, $3$-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have $\isContr(\NNO)$, we automatically get $\isContr(\Omega^{n}\NNO)$ for every $n$, by general HoTT lemmas.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1454:364:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1459:432:  \item There exists an $(\infty,1)$-NNO in $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{def:lurie-nno}).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1460:440:By Shulman's theorem, $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT in which $\U$ is interpreted as the object classifier and the type $\NNO$ of \cref{def:nno-type} is interpreted as an internal object of $\mathcal{E}$ (more precisely, as the underlying object of an $\infty$-groupoid object of $\mathcal{E}$, classified by $\U$). By \cref{thm:contr-hott}, this internal type is contractible (i.e.\ the proposition $\isContr(\NNO)$ has a global element).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1462:463:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of $\Sone$ coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based $(\infty,1)$-NNO in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno} (when both are defined).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1463:467:By part~(2) of \cref{thm:main}, $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh's NNO (\cref{thm:rasekh}) and any Lurie-style NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) are points of $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1466:523:A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of~\cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT\@. We are not aware of a published proof.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1476:166:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1477:238:\Cref{lem:rigid} provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid $\mathbf{1}$, hence contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1478:284:\Cref{thm:contr-1cat} says ``unique iso between any two NNOs''. \Cref{thm:contr-hott} says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of $2$-paths is contractible, and so on. In a $1$-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an $\infty$-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because $\isContr$ is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1481:149:By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1482:151:The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1484:257:\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of $\NNO$ in HoTT~\cite{PaperIII,PaperV}]\label{thm:contr-hott}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1486:339:A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1489:442:\emph{Externalisation.} The functor $\mathcal{E}\to \Spc$, $X\mapsto \Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)$, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Internal contractibility of $\NNO$ in the HoTT model is the global element of $\isContr(\NNO)$, which under externalisation becomes a global point of $\Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\isContr\,\NNO)\eqv\isContr(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))$, witnessing that $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible in $\Spc$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1492:520:where $\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}$ uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1493:523:A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of~\cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT\@. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1497:559:The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?'' The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1784:109:Contractibility, $\infty$-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1799:124:The natural numbers object (NNO) of an elementary topos is, classically, an object equipped with a global element $0$ and an endomorphism $s$ such that the resulting pointed dynamical system is initial. In a $1$-topos any two NNOs are connected by a unique isomorphism, and the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial: NNO structures form a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated $\infty$-groupoid. In homotopy type theory (HoTT), Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III (and the equivalent Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V of the present series) refines this rigidity: the type
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1801:126:\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1803:128:is contractible. By Shulman's interpretation theorem, every $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT~+~univalence, so the corresponding statement transports to an internal contractibility result for the type of NNO structures inside $\mathcal{E}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1805:130:This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1816:141:The natural numbers object (NNO) is among the most basic universal constructions of categorical logic. Lawvere's 1964 axiomatisation of the elementary theory of the category of sets (ETCS) treats the NNO as an irreducible primitive on equal footing with the terminal object, the subobject classifier, and exponentials. In any cartesian closed category $\mathcal{C}$ with a terminal object $\mathbf{1}$, an NNO is a triple $(N, 0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N, s\colon N\to N)$ such that for every $(X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)$ there exists a unique morphism $h\colon N\to X$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$. Two NNOs are related by a unique isomorphism; the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial. In $\Set$ the NNO is the von Neumann set $\omega = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ with successor; up to canonical isomorphism, ``the natural numbers'' is a property, not a choice of structure.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1820:145:\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1822:147:where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1824:149:By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1826:151:The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1831:156:  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1832:157:  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1833:158:  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1834:159:  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1835:160:  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1841:166:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1847:172:\section{Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility}\label{sec:background}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1851:176:\subsection{Pointed endomorphisms and \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1857:182:\begin{definition}[NNO]\label{def:nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1861:186:Equivalently, viewing the endofunctor $F\colon \mathcal{C}\to \mathcal{C}$ given by $FX = \mathbf{1}+X$ (when coproducts exist), an NNO is an initial $F$-algebra: a pair $(N,[0,s]\colon \mathbf{1}+N\to N)$ initial in $F\text{-}\mathbf{Alg}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1898:223:\begin{theorem}[Universal property of NNO]\label{thm:up}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1899:224:Let $(N,0,s)$ be an NNO in $\mathcal{C}$. For every $(X,x_{0},f)$ in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{C})$ there exists a unique $h\colon N\to X$ in $\mathcal{C}$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1902:227:\subsection{1-Categorical contractibility (rigidity of NNOs)}\label{sec:rigidity-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1906:231:In $\Set$, the NNO is $(\N,0,\mathrm{succ})$. By \cref{lem:rigid} the groupoid of NNOs in $\Set$ has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated space.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1908:233:\begin{theorem}[1-categorical contractibility of $\NNO$]\label{thm:contr-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1909:234:In any category $\mathcal{C}$ with terminal object, the groupoid $\NNO(\mathcal{C})$ of NNO structures is either empty or contractible (i.e.\ a $(-2)$-type). Equivalently, the moduli space of NNOs in $\mathcal{C}$ is a (mere) proposition: existence implies essential uniqueness, with no nontrivial automorphisms or higher cells (which are absent at the $1$-categorical level).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1913:238:\Cref{lem:rigid} provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid $\mathbf{1}$, hence contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1920:245:\begin{definition}[Type of NNOs in HoTT]\label{def:nno-type}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1922:247:\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1932:257:\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of $\NNO$ in HoTT~\cite{PaperIII,PaperV}]\label{thm:contr-hott}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1933:258:The type $\NNO$ is contractible. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical witness of initiality given by the standard recursor $\rec_{\N}$ together with its computation rules.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1937:262:We must show $\isContr(\NNO)$, i.e.\ produce a centre $c\in\NNO$ and a continuous family of paths from $c$ to every point of $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1945:270:\emph{Contraction onto the centre.} Take any $(X,x_{0},s,\iota)\in\NNO$. We must produce a path
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1947:272:p\colon (\N,\zero,\suc,\star) \;=\; (X,x_{0},s,\iota) \quad\text{in}\quad \NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1949:274:Apply $\iota$ to the pointed system $(\N,\zero,\suc)$: it yields a contractible type whose centre is a unique map $u\colon X\to \N$ with $u\,x_{0}=\zero$ and $u\circ s\sim \suc\circ u$. Apply $\star$ to $(X,x_{0},s)$: a unique map $v\colon \N\to X$ with $v\,\zero=x_{0}$ and $v\circ \suc\sim s\circ v$. The composites $u\circ v\colon \N\to\N$ and $\id_{\N}$ both satisfy the universal-property equations for $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$; uniqueness inside the contractible type of recursors forces $u\circ v=\id_{\N}$ via $\ind_{\N}$, and likewise $v\circ u=\id_{X}$ via $\iota$. Thus $u\colon X\eqv\N$ is an equivalence (with quasi-inverse $v$). Univalence ($\ua$) converts the equivalence into a path $p_{1}\colon X = \N$ in $\U$. Transporting $(x_{0},s,\iota)$ along $p_{1}$ produces $(\zero,\suc,\star')$ for some witness $\star'\colon \mathsf{isInitial}(\N,\zero,\suc)$. Since $\mathsf{isInitial}$ is a proposition, $\star'=\star$. Pairing yields the required path $p$ in $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1955:280:The proof of \cref{thm:contr-hott} bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact $\isContr(\NNO)$. Path induction (the $J$-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, $2$-paths, $3$-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have $\isContr(\NNO)$, we automatically get $\isContr(\Omega^{n}\NNO)$ for every $n$, by general HoTT lemmas.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1959:284:\Cref{thm:contr-1cat} says ``unique iso between any two NNOs''. \Cref{thm:contr-hott} says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of $2$-paths is contractible, and so on. In a $1$-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an $\infty$-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because $\isContr$ is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1962:287:\section{The \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-categorical NNO}\label{sec:lurie}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1966:291:\subsection{Lurie's parametrised NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1968:293:\begin{definition}[$(\infty,1)$-NNO, Lurie]\label{def:lurie-nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1969:294:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos with terminal object $\mathbf{1}$. An NNO is an object $N\in\mathcal{E}$ together with morphisms $0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N$ and $s\colon N\to N$ such that for every diagram $\mathbf{1}\xrightarrow{x_{0}}X\xrightarrow{f}X$ in $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1983:308:Equivalently, an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is an initial object in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$, where ``initial'' is interpreted in the $(\infty,1)$-categorical sense: the mapping space to every other object is contractible. Lurie phrases the definition via a small-colimit-preserving recursion functor; the equivalence is straightforward.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1987:312:When $\mathcal{E}=\Spc$, the NNO is the constant functor at $\N$. Its existence and contractibility of the mapping space is the homotopy-coherent version of the Set-theoretic recursion principle.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1991:316:For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})$ the $(\infty,1)$-category of presheaves of spaces on a small $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{C}$, the NNO is the constant presheaf at $\N$, $c\mapsto \N$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:1995:320:For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ the $(\infty,1)$-topos of sheaves on a topological space $X$, the NNO is the constant sheaf $\underline{\N}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2001:326:Every presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2012:337:\subsection{Elementary \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-toposes and Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2014:339:A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2025:350:Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO\@. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2029:354:The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO\@. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2033:358:Rasekh's proof is striking because it derives a discrete, $0$-truncated, NNO from a manifestly $1$-truncated object, $\Sone$, via genuinely $\infty$-categorical machinery. There is no $1$-categorical analogue: $\Omega\Sone$ in the $1$-topos of sets is a singleton.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2036:361:\subsection{Equivalence with the \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO when \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{E}$}{E} is a \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-topos}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2039:364:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2052:377:Why is the $(\infty,1)$-NNO ``more delicate''? The naive answer is: in a $1$-category, ``unique map'' is a single condition. In an $(\infty,1)$-category, ``unique map'' must be promoted to ``unique up to coherent homotopy''. Concretely, given two NNOs $N_{1}, N_{2}$ in $\mathcal{E}$, we need a comparison map $u\colon N_{1}\to N_{2}$ together with the homotopies filling the squares
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2067:392:The initiality of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO already encodes, in a single property, the entire infinite tower of coherence data sketched above. Concretely, for any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ in an $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2075:400:By definition of $(\infty,1)$-NNO, both $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ are initial objects of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$. Initial objects in any $(\infty,1)$-category have contractible mapping spaces to every object, in particular to each other. Contractibility of $M$ is equivalent to existence of a $0$-cell (the comparison map $u$ and the homotopies $\alpha,\beta$), uniqueness up to a contractible space of $1$-cells (the next-level coherences), uniqueness of those up to a contractible space of $2$-cells, and so on inductively at every level $n\geq 0$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2086:411:\subsection{Truncation level of \texorpdfstring{$\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$}{NNO(E)}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2088:413:Define $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ as the full subspace of the $\infty$-groupoid $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})^{\eqv}$ (the maximal sub-$\infty$-groupoid of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$) on objects that are initial.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2090:415:\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of the $\infty$-NNO space]\label{thm:contractibility-infty}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2091:416:For any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ in which an NNO exists, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2098:423:\section{The main theorem: contractibility for \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-NNOs}\label{sec:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2104:429:\begin{theorem}[Main theorem]\label{thm:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2107:432:  \item There exists an $(\infty,1)$-NNO in $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{def:lurie-nno}).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2108:433:  \item The space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible (a $(-2)$-type).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2109:434:  \item Any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs in $\mathcal{E}$ are connected by an essentially unique equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2110:435:  \item The automorphism $\infty$-group of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2115:440:By Shulman's theorem, $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT in which $\U$ is interpreted as the object classifier and the type $\NNO$ of \cref{def:nno-type} is interpreted as an internal object of $\mathcal{E}$ (more precisely, as the underlying object of an $\infty$-groupoid object of $\mathcal{E}$, classified by $\U$). By \cref{thm:contr-hott}, this internal type is contractible (i.e.\ the proposition $\isContr(\NNO)$ has a global element).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2117:442:\emph{Externalisation.} The functor $\mathcal{E}\to \Spc$, $X\mapsto \Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)$, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Internal contractibility of $\NNO$ in the HoTT model is the global element of $\isContr(\NNO)$, which under externalisation becomes a global point of $\Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\isContr\,\NNO)\eqv\isContr(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))$, witnessing that $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible in $\Spc$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2135:460:\subsection{Identification with Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2138:463:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of $\Sone$ coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based $(\infty,1)$-NNO in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno} (when both are defined).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2142:467:By part~(2) of \cref{thm:main}, $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh's NNO (\cref{thm:rasekh}) and any Lurie-style NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) are points of $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2145:470:\section{Rasekh's circle-construction of the NNO}\label{sec:rasekh}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2178:503:In an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos with object classifier $\U$, the NNO can be constructed without separate appeal to power objects.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2189:514:\subsection{An NNO in STT}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2193:518:\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}} \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X}\,\Sigmatype_{s\colon X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2195:520:where $\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}$ uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2198:523:A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of~\cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT\@. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2209:534:For the NNO, all three internal languages give equivalent answers when restricted to discrete types, but only the first two have published contractibility theorems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2215:540:  Make the externalisation step in the proof of \cref{thm:main} fully precise. This includes coherent strictification of the internal type $\NNO$ to an external $\infty$-groupoid.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2218:543:  \item \textbf{Synthetic NNO contractibility in STT.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2219:544:  Prove that $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ is contractible in simplicial type theory, using only Rezk-completeness of the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2222:547:  \item \textbf{Lurie's parametrised NNO.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2234:559:The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?'' The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2238:563:In the modular framework adopted by the broader research programme, the NNO sits at the bottom of a tower:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2240:565:  \item \textbf{NNO} (this paper): provides arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2246:571:The contractibility of NNO at the $(\infty,1)$-level ensures that everything built on top of NNO inherits a unique, canonical foundation. This is the modular composition principle: each layer is uniquely determined by the previous one, up to contractible choice.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2253:578:  \item The connection to Rasekh's circle-construction is via contractibility: we know the two NNOs agree, but we do not give an explicit comparison map.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2262:587:  \item the $1$-categorical NNO and its contractibility (\cref{thm:contr-1cat});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2263:588:  \item Lurie's parametrised $(\infty,1)$-NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) and its existence for presentable $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{thm:exist-pres});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2264:589:  \item Rasekh's circle-construction of an NNO in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos (\cref{thm:rasekh});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2265:590:  \item the main contractibility theorem (\cref{thm:main}) showing $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever HoTT models in $\mathcal{E}$;
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2329:654:\bibitem{nLabNNO} The nLab, \emph{natural numbers object},\ \url{https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/natural+numbers+object}, accessed 2026.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2339:This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.  
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2341:Warning 13 in papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex line 166: Intersentence spacing (`\@') should perhaps be used.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2342:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2426:The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?'' The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''  
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2428:Warning 38 in papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex line 559: You should not use punctuation in front of quotes.

The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''

The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2441:130:This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2443:160:  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2444:166:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2446:326:Every presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2447:543:  \item \textbf{Synthetic NNO contractibility in STT.}
