./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2451:/usr/local/bin/zsh -lc "rg -n \"\\\\@\\.\" papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex" in /Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2454:350:Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO\@. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2455:354:The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO\@. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2457:523:A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of~\cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT\@. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2460:/usr/local/bin/zsh -lc 'rg -n "''^[[:space:]]*$|''^.{121,}$" papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex' in /Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2480:124:The natural numbers object (NNO) of an elementary topos is, classically, an object equipped with a global element $0$ and an endomorphism $s$ such that the resulting pointed dynamical system is initial. In a $1$-topos any two NNOs are connected by a unique isomorphism, and the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial: NNO structures form a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated $\infty$-groupoid. In homotopy type theory (HoTT), Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III (and the equivalent Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V of the present series) refines this rigidity: the type
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2481:128:is contractible. By Shulman's interpretation theorem, every $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT~+~univalence, so the corresponding statement transports to an internal contractibility result for the type of NNO structures inside $\mathcal{E}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2483:130:This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2490:141:The natural numbers object (NNO) is among the most basic universal constructions of categorical logic. Lawvere's 1964 axiomatisation of the elementary theory of the category of sets (ETCS) treats the NNO as an irreducible primitive on equal footing with the terminal object, the subobject classifier, and exponentials. In any cartesian closed category $\mathcal{C}$ with a terminal object $\mathbf{1}$, an NNO is a triple $(N, 0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N, s\colon N\to N)$ such that for every $(X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)$ there exists a unique morphism $h\colon N\to X$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$. Two NNOs are related by a unique isomorphism; the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial. In $\Set$ the NNO is the von Neumann set $\omega = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ with successor; up to canonical isomorphism, ``the natural numbers'' is a property, not a choice of structure.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2492:147:where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2494:149:By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2496:151:The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2499:156:  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2500:157:  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2501:158:  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2502:159:  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2503:160:  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2507:166:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2518:186:Equivalently, viewing the endofunctor $F\colon \mathcal{C}\to \mathcal{C}$ given by $FX = \mathbf{1}+X$ (when coproducts exist), an NNO is an initial $F$-algebra: a pair $(N,[0,s]\colon \mathbf{1}+N\to N)$ initial in $F\text{-}\mathbf{Alg}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2529:224:Let $(N,0,s)$ be an NNO in $\mathcal{C}$. For every $(X,x_{0},f)$ in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{C})$ there exists a unique $h\colon N\to X$ in $\mathcal{C}$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2534:231:In $\Set$, the NNO is $(\N,0,\mathrm{succ})$. By \cref{lem:rigid} the groupoid of NNOs in $\Set$ has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated space.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2536:234:In any category $\mathcal{C}$ with terminal object, the groupoid $\NNO(\mathcal{C})$ of NNO structures is either empty or contractible (i.e.\ a $(-2)$-type). Equivalently, the moduli space of NNOs in $\mathcal{C}$ is a (mere) proposition: existence implies essential uniqueness, with no nontrivial automorphisms or higher cells (which are absent at the $1$-categorical level).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2538:238:\Cref{lem:rigid} provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid $\mathbf{1}$, hence contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2547:258:The type $\NNO$ is contractible. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical witness of initiality given by the standard recursor $\rec_{\N}$ together with its computation rules.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2549:262:We must show $\isContr(\NNO)$, i.e.\ produce a centre $c\in\NNO$ and a continuous family of paths from $c$ to every point of $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2554:274:Apply $\iota$ to the pointed system $(\N,\zero,\suc)$: it yields a contractible type whose centre is a unique map $u\colon X\to \N$ with $u\,x_{0}=\zero$ and $u\circ s\sim \suc\circ u$. Apply $\star$ to $(X,x_{0},s)$: a unique map $v\colon \N\to X$ with $v\,\zero=x_{0}$ and $v\circ \suc\sim s\circ v$. The composites $u\circ v\colon \N\to\N$ and $\id_{\N}$ both satisfy the universal-property equations for $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$; uniqueness inside the contractible type of recursors forces $u\circ v=\id_{\N}$ via $\ind_{\N}$, and likewise $v\circ u=\id_{X}$ via $\iota$. Thus $u\colon X\eqv\N$ is an equivalence (with quasi-inverse $v$). Univalence ($\ua$) converts the equivalence into a path $p_{1}\colon X = \N$ in $\U$. Transporting $(x_{0},s,\iota)$ along $p_{1}$ produces $(\zero,\suc,\star')$ for some witness $\star'\colon \mathsf{isInitial}(\N,\zero,\suc)$. Since $\mathsf{isInitial}$ is a proposition, $\star'=\star$. Pairing yields the required path $p$ in $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2558:280:The proof of \cref{thm:contr-hott} bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact $\isContr(\NNO)$. Path induction (the $J$-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, $2$-paths, $3$-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have $\isContr(\NNO)$, we automatically get $\isContr(\Omega^{n}\NNO)$ for every $n$, by general HoTT lemmas.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2560:284:\Cref{thm:contr-1cat} says ``unique iso between any two NNOs''. \Cref{thm:contr-hott} says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of $2$-paths is contractible, and so on. In a $1$-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an $\infty$-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because $\isContr$ is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2566:294:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos with terminal object $\mathbf{1}$. An NNO is an object $N\in\mathcal{E}$ together with morphisms $0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N$ and $s\colon N\to N$ such that for every diagram $\mathbf{1}\xrightarrow{x_{0}}X\xrightarrow{f}X$ in $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2570:308:Equivalently, an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is an initial object in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$, where ``initial'' is interpreted in the $(\infty,1)$-categorical sense: the mapping space to every other object is contractible. Lurie phrases the definition via a small-colimit-preserving recursion functor; the equivalence is straightforward.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2572:312:When $\mathcal{E}=\Spc$, the NNO is the constant functor at $\N$. Its existence and contractibility of the mapping space is the homotopy-coherent version of the Set-theoretic recursion principle.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2574:316:For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})$ the $(\infty,1)$-category of presheaves of spaces on a small $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{C}$, the NNO is the constant presheaf at $\N$, $c\mapsto \N$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2576:320:For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ the $(\infty,1)$-topos of sheaves on a topological space $X$, the NNO is the constant sheaf $\underline{\N}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2584:339:A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2586:350:Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO\@. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2588:354:The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO\@. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2590:358:Rasekh's proof is striking because it derives a discrete, $0$-truncated, NNO from a manifestly $1$-truncated object, $\Sone$, via genuinely $\infty$-categorical machinery. There is no $1$-categorical analogue: $\Omega\Sone$ in the $1$-topos of sets is a singleton.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2592:361:\subsection{Equivalence with the \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO when \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{E}$}{E} is a \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-topos}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2594:364:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2599:377:Why is the $(\infty,1)$-NNO ``more delicate''? The naive answer is: in a $1$-category, ``unique map'' is a single condition. In an $(\infty,1)$-category, ``unique map'' must be promoted to ``unique up to coherent homotopy''. Concretely, given two NNOs $N_{1}, N_{2}$ in $\mathcal{E}$, we need a comparison map $u\colon N_{1}\to N_{2}$ together with the homotopies filling the squares
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2603:392:The initiality of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO already encodes, in a single property, the entire infinite tower of coherence data sketched above. Concretely, for any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ in an $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2605:400:By definition of $(\infty,1)$-NNO, both $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ are initial objects of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$. Initial objects in any $(\infty,1)$-category have contractible mapping spaces to every object, in particular to each other. Contractibility of $M$ is equivalent to existence of a $0$-cell (the comparison map $u$ and the homotopies $\alpha,\beta$), uniqueness up to a contractible space of $1$-cells (the next-level coherences), uniqueness of those up to a contractible space of $2$-cells, and so on inductively at every level $n\geq 0$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2610:413:Define $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ as the full subspace of the $\infty$-groupoid $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})^{\eqv}$ (the maximal sub-$\infty$-groupoid of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$) on objects that are initial.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2621:440:By Shulman's theorem, $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT in which $\U$ is interpreted as the object classifier and the type $\NNO$ of \cref{def:nno-type} is interpreted as an internal object of $\mathcal{E}$ (more precisely, as the underlying object of an $\infty$-groupoid object of $\mathcal{E}$, classified by $\U$). By \cref{thm:contr-hott}, this internal type is contractible (i.e.\ the proposition $\isContr(\NNO)$ has a global element).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2623:442:\emph{Externalisation.} The functor $\mathcal{E}\to \Spc$, $X\mapsto \Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)$, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Internal contractibility of $\NNO$ in the HoTT model is the global element of $\isContr(\NNO)$, which under externalisation becomes a global point of $\Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\isContr\,\NNO)\eqv\isContr(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))$, witnessing that $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible in $\Spc$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2632:463:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of $\Sone$ coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based $(\infty,1)$-NNO in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno} (when both are defined).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2634:467:By part~(2) of \cref{thm:main}, $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh's NNO (\cref{thm:rasekh}) and any Lurie-style NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) are points of $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2653:503:In an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos with object classifier $\U$, the NNO can be constructed without separate appeal to power objects.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2660:518:\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}} \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X}\,\Sigmatype_{s\colon X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2661:520:where $\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}$ uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2663:523:A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of~\cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT\@. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2668:534:For the NNO, all three internal languages give equivalent answers when restricted to discrete types, but only the first two have published contractibility theorems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2671:540:  Make the externalisation step in the proof of \cref{thm:main} fully precise. This includes coherent strictification of the internal type $\NNO$ to an external $\infty$-groupoid.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2673:544:  Prove that $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ is contractible in simplicial type theory, using only Rezk-completeness of the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2681:559:The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?'' The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible.''
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2684:571:The contractibility of NNO at the $(\infty,1)$-level ensures that everything built on top of NNO inherits a unique, canonical foundation. This is the modular composition principle: each layer is uniquely determined by the previous one, up to contractible choice.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2689:578:  \item The connection to Rasekh's circle-construction is via contractibility: we know the two NNOs agree, but we do not give an explicit comparison map.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2694:588:  \item Lurie's parametrised $(\infty,1)$-NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) and its existence for presentable $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{thm:exist-pres});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2695:590:  \item the main contractibility theorem (\cref{thm:main}) showing $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever HoTT models in $\mathcal{E}$;
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review.md:2751:654:\bibitem{nLabNNO} The nLab, \emph{natural numbers object},\ \url{https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/natural+numbers+object}, accessed 2026.

./docs/papers/synthesis.html:9:<li><p>primitive recursion / iteration on <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span>, hence an NNO whose universal property is correctly stated in the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical context where holomorphic functions live (Part IV);</p></li>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:10:<li><p>a constructive analysis library that respects the inductive / coinductive duality (Part I), since power series and Dirichlet series are inductively-defined limits of coinductively-presented streams of partial sums;</p></li>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:18:<p><strong>From <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}\)</span> to <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}, \mathbb{C}_{c}, \zeta\)</span>.</strong> The prior series developed the infrastructure (NNOs, HoTT, univalence, structuralism) but applied it mostly to discrete objects (natural numbers, finite groups, <span class="math inline">\(\pi_1(S^1)\)</span>). The principal continuous object whose HoTT presentation it discussed was the Cauchy reals <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span>, but only as a HIIT in Book HoTT, and only for the purpose of defining <span class="math inline">\(\pi\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(e\)</span> as centres of contractible subtypes (Paper V §8). The present series goes further: a complete coalgebraic dual for transcendentals (Part I), a cubical formalisation of the HIIT itself (Part II), and the analytic objects — complex numbers, holomorphic functions, Dirichlet series, <span class="math inline">\(L\)</span>-functions — needed to write down <span class="math inline">\(\zeta\)</span> (Part VI).</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:19:<p><strong>From 1-categorical to <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical.</strong> The prior series worked mostly in 1-categorical universe, citing Lurie and Riehl–Shulman where necessary but not developing the higher-categorical theory. The present series tightens the higher-categorical frame: contractibility of the type of <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical NNOs (Part IV), directed univalence of the universe of discrete types (Part V), and a complete recasting of the Riemann zeta function as an internal object of an <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos (Part VI §4–5).</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:82:<li><p><span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{cu}}\)</span> is the univalent universe of types in cubical type theory (Part II), with both inductive HIITs and coinductive M-types (Parts I, II) and an NNO whose contractibility is internalised at the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-level (Part IV);</p></li>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:92:<li><p>Part IV: certifies that the NNO needed for the Dirichlet sum <span class="math inline">\(\sum_{n \geq 1} n^{-s}\)</span> in the <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical universe is contractibly unique.</p></li>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:148:<h1 data-number="6" id="sec:partIV"><span class="header-section-number">6</span> Part IV: <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNOs</h1>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:150:<p>Part IV <span class="citation" data-cites="infinityNno"></span> addresses the open problem (Synthesis §8 item 4) of the higher-categorical structure of NNO. The prior series proved contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (Paper V Theorem 4.4). The <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical analogue, in elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes, is more delicate: Rasekh <span class="citation" data-cites="Rasekh2018"></span> showed that every elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos has a NNO, by constructing it from the loop space of <span class="math inline">\(S^1 = K(\mathbb{Z},1)\)</span>.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:153:<p><strong>Theorem 9</strong> (Higher contractibility, <span class="citation" data-cites="infinityNno"></span>, Theorem 4.4 / <span class="citation" data-cites="Rasekh2018"></span>).  <em>The space <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}_{(\infty,1)}\)</span> of natural-numbers objects in any elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-topos <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{E}\)</span> is contractible: the canonical forgetful map <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{NNO}_{(\infty,1)} \to \mathcal{E}_*\)</span> to pointed objects is an inclusion of a contractible subspace.</em></p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:156:<p><strong>Theorem 10</strong> (Lurie / Rasekh equivalence).  <em>Lurie’s NNO (in presentable <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes) and Rasekh’s NNO (in elementary <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-toposes) coincide on their common domain of definition; the higher coherences of the universal property follow automatically from contractibility.</em></p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:161:<p>Part IV uses Part II’s cubical machinery to formalise contractibility within Cubical Agda, and feeds Part VI’s <span class="math inline">\(\zeta\)</span>. It extends Paper III §3 (NNO universal property), Paper V Theorem 4.4 (contractibility in HoTT), and Paper VI Theorem 4.1 (initial in <span class="math inline">\(\mathrm{Mod}(T_{\mathrm{succ}})\)</span>).</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:221:<p>This is not vacuous. In ZFC, “the natural numbers” is not a single object: there are von Neumann naturals, Zermelo naturals, and infinitely many other encodings, all with different junk theorems (Paper II §4–6). In HoTT, “the natural numbers” is a single type <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}: \mathcal{U}\)</span>, and the type of NNO structures is contractible (Paper V Theorem 4.4 / Part IV). Univalence is the principle that makes the category-theoretic “up to isomorphism” literally an identity.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:223:<p>The bridge from 1-categorical universal properties (“unique up to isomorphism”) to <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical ones (“unique up to contractible space of choices”) is the contractibility theorem of Part IV. The 1-categorical statement says: any two NNOs are uniquely isomorphic. The <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-categorical statement says: the space of NNOs is contractible, i.e. uniquely isomorphic in a way that is itself unique up to a unique 2-isomorphism, etc.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:262:<p><strong>Apparatus required.</strong> Gates 1, 2; Part I (coalgebraic streams of partial sums); Part IV (NNO indexing). Note the natural use of <a href="#prin:dual" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="prin:dual">13</a>: convergence is coinductive over the partial-sum stream, while the algebra structure is inductive on the underlying sequences.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:326:<td style="text-align: left;">Part IV (<span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO)</td>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:398:<p><strong>(3) Computation comes for free.</strong> In Cubical Agda (Part II), the construction of <span class="math inline">\(\zeta\)</span> would be <em>executable</em>: one could ask the proof assistant to compute <span class="math inline">\(\zeta(2)\)</span> to <span class="math inline">\(k\)</span> decimal places, and the construction of the proof of <span class="math inline">\(\zeta(2) = \pi^2/6\)</span> would yield, as a byproduct, an algorithm computing <span class="math inline">\(\pi^2/6\)</span> to <span class="math inline">\(k\)</span> decimal places, by Type II Turing computability of the cubical reals.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:403:<li><p>The infrastructure — HIIT machinery (Part II), <span class="math inline">\((\infty,1)\)</span>-NNO (Part IV), directed univalence (Part V) — is itself a research-level investment.</p></li>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:417:<p>The estimates in <a href="#sec:zeta-roadmap" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:zeta-roadmap">10</a> assume Cubical Agda as the implementation platform. Other platforms are possible: Lean 4 with a HoTT layer (in development), Coq with the UniMath library, or rzk <span class="citation" data-cites="rzk"></span> for the directed-univalent fragment. Whether the cubical flavour or the rzk flavour is better suited to the directed parts (Gate 5, Part V) is an empirical question whose answer awaits experimentation. A pragmatic strategy might combine the two: cubical Agda for the symmetric-univalent fragment and rzk for the directed fragment, glued via an interface based on the universe of discrete types.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:421:<li><p>a Cubical Agda library defining <span class="math inline">\(\zeta : \mathbb{C}_{c}\to \mathbb{C}_{c}\)</span> with the Euler product, the functional equation, and the formal RH statement;</p></li>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:435:<p><strong>Open Problem 25</strong>.  Implement a Cubical Agda library for analytic continuation of holomorphic functions on <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{C}_{c}\)</span>. The library should include the identity theorem and the monodromy theorem in their constructive forms.</p>
./docs/papers/synthesis.html:464:<p>M. Long, “Higher-Categorical Natural Numbers Objects: Contractibility, <span class="math inline">\(\infty\)</span>-Toposes, and Lurie’s NNO.” GrokRxiv:2026.05.infinity-nno, 4 May 2026. (Part IV)</p>
./reviews/coalgebraic-transcendentals-review-round-4.md:35:3.  **(Completeness) Haskell/Lean Artifacts:** The conclusion mentions accompanying Haskell and Lean 4 files. While this is a review of the paper itself, it is worth noting that the claim of having a Lean artifact formalising the coinduction principle as "classical, set-level" is slightly at odds with the paper's emphasis on working within constructive, cubical HoTT. This is a very minor point of internal consistency between the paper's narrative and its claimed supplementary materials. A more HoTT-aligned formalisation would presumably use a cubical library. This does not detract from the paper's quality but is a small point of friction.
./docs/papers/directed-univalence.html:337:<p><strong>Proposition 43</strong> (NNO in discrete directed type theory).  <em>In <span class="math inline">\(\mathbf{TTT}\)</span>, the natural numbers <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{N}: \mathcal{S}\)</span> are characterised up to equivalence by initiality: <span class="math display">\[\mathsf{isContr}\Big( \sum_{f : \mathbb{N}\to A} f(0) =_A a_0, \;\text{compatible with succ} \Big)\]</span> for any pointed endo-discrete-type <span class="math inline">\((A, a_0, s)\)</span>. Directed univalence (Theorem <a href="#thm:dua-s" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:dua-s">22</a>) then identifies these initiality witnesses with directed morphisms in <span class="math inline">\(\mathcal{S}\)</span>.</em></p>

./docs/papers/langlands-analytic-number-theory.html:572:<li><p>A large prebuilt library of measure theory, integration, and complex analysis.</p></li>
./docs/papers/langlands-analytic-number-theory.html:662:<li><p><code>Main.hs</code> — driver illustrating partial <span class="math inline">\(\zeta\)</span> sums and Dirichlet series operations.</p></li>
./docs/papers/langlands-analytic-number-theory.html:665:<li><p><code>Properties.hs</code> — QuickCheck properties for the algebraic laws on Dirichlet series.</p></li>
./docs/papers/langlands-analytic-number-theory.html:666:<li><p><code>Proofs.hs</code> — equational proofs of the Euler product identity, modulo a finite-truncation axiom.</p></li>
./docs/papers/langlands-analytic-number-theory.html:672:<p>These are illustrative prototypes, not formal verifications. Their purpose is to make the prerequisite chain (<a href="#sec:prereq" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:prereq">2</a>) and the candidate definitions (<a href="#sec:zeta" data-reference-type="ref+Label" data-reference="sec:zeta">3</a>) executable, so that future HoTT-native work has computational benchmarks.</p>

./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:2:<p>The constructive real numbers occupy a privileged position in the foundations of analysis. In a setting without the law of excluded middle, no canonical definition of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}\)</span> exists; instead, several non-equivalent candidates compete: <em>Cauchy reals</em> <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span>, defined as a quotient of Cauchy sequences of rationals; <em>Dedekind reals</em> <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{d}\)</span>, defined as located cuts; and <em>streams</em> or <em>redundant binary signed-digit representations</em>, definable as final coalgebras (Paper III, §5). In classical mathematics these collapse to a single object, but constructively they differ by countable choice, fan-theorem-like principles, or modulus-of-convergence considerations <span class="citation" data-cites="BridgesRichman"></span>. The <em>Cauchy</em> presentation is the most useful for executable analysis: it admits a direct definition of limits, supports the unique-existence definitions of transcendentals via Picard-style fixed points, and yields an Archimedean ordered field on the nose.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:20:<p>The remainder of the paper makes (R1)–(R4) precise, gives the Cubical Agda code for the central definitions and theorems, and identifies the remaining gaps—chiefly the absence of a fully judgemental <span class="math inline">\(\eta\)</span>-rule for the closeness constructor and the lack of an integrated <code>Cubical.HITs.CauchyReals</code> module in the standard library <span class="citation" data-cites="CubicalAgdaLib"></span>.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:165:<p>Cubical Agda <span class="citation" data-cites="CubicalAgda"></span> is a mode of the Agda proof assistant that natively supports CCHM-style operations. The interval is a sort <code>I</code>; <code>Path</code> and <code>PathP</code> are primitive; <code>hcomp</code> and <code>transp</code> reduce. The standard library |cubical/cubical| <span class="citation" data-cites="CubicalAgdaLib"></span> provides set quotients (|Cubical.HITs.SetQuotients|), propositional truncation (|Cubical.HITs.PropositionalTruncation|), and several other HITs.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:442:<p>In the Cubical Agda code, evaluating <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{approx}_{1/100}\,\sqrt{2}\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(\sqrt{2}\)</span> is defined as the centre of the contractible type <span class="math inline">\(\Sigma_{x : \mathbb{R}_{c}}\,(x \cdot x = 2) \times (x &gt; 0)\)</span>, reduces to a specific rational. The terms <span class="math inline">\(\pi\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(e\)</span> used here are the Cubical Agda implementations of the unique-existence definitions recalled in Section <a href="#sec:hott-recap" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:hott-recap">3</a> (Definitions <a href="#def:pi" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="def:pi">5</a> and <a href="#def:e" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="def:e">6</a>): <span class="math inline">\(\pi\)</span> is the centre of <span class="math inline">\(P_{\pi}\)</span>, and <span class="math inline">\(e\)</span> is <span class="math inline">\(\exp\,1\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(\exp\)</span> is the centre of <span class="math inline">\(P_{\exp}\)</span>. The functions <span class="math inline">\(\sin\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(\exp\)</span> on which those definitions depend are themselves defined cubically as centres of contractible types of solutions to their characteristic ODEs; their computational basis in Cubical Agda is the standard Picard-style fixed-point construction, ported via Theorem <a href="#thm:Rc-univ" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:Rc-univ">15</a>’s universal property. We give some sample extracts (computed by reduction in Cubical Agda; verified via the Haskell prototype in <code>src/cubical-hiit-cauchy/Main.hs</code>):</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:476:<h2 data-number="10.2" id="full-iit-schema-in-the-standard-library"><span class="header-section-number">10.2</span> Full IIT Schema in the Standard Library</h2>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:477:<p>The construction of Section <a href="#sec:cubical-cauchy" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:cubical-cauchy">6</a> uses an <em>ad-hoc</em> mutual block. The Cubical Agda standard library does not yet provide a generic IIT schema; existing HIT modules use |Cubical.Codata.Stream| or |Cubical.HITs.SetQuotients| as single-layer constructions. A clean |Cubical.HITs.CauchyReals| module is one of our deliverables.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:481:<p>The agda-unimath library <span class="citation" data-cites="AgdaUnimath"></span> formalises algebraic structures (rings, fields, polynomial rings) in Cubical Agda. Lifting the cubical <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span> to an Archimedean ordered field in agda-unimath would allow direct use in their formalisation of Lindemann–Weierstrass and related transcendence theorems (cf. Paper V §8.3).</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:487:<h2 data-number="10.7" id="towards-a-standard-library-module"><span class="header-section-number">10.7</span> Towards a Standard Library Module</h2>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:488:<p>We propose the following organisation for a future |Cubical.HITs.CauchyReals| module in the cubical/cubical standard library:</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:491:<li><p><strong>Properties.</strong> h-set, Archimedean, complete (Theorems <a href="#thm:Rc-isSet" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:Rc-isSet">14</a>, <a href="#thm:archimedean" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:archimedean">24</a>, <a href="#thm:complete" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:complete">25</a>).</p></li>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:503:<li><p><strong>Type-classes.</strong> An alternative is to define <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span> as the underlying type of a type-class for “Cauchy-complete Archimedean ordered fields,” as in Coq’s MathClasses library <span class="citation" data-cites="MathClasses"></span>. This is non-canonical: many such fields exist classically (e.g. the computable reals, the Markov reals). The HIIT presentation isolates the canonical Cauchy completion of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{Q}\)</span>.</p></li>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:511:<p>First, the construction is given as a <em>specification</em> in Cubical Agda; we do not yet ship a complete, type-checked implementation inside the cubical/cubical standard library. This is a deliberate choice: the goal of the paper is to settle the design questions (which Book HoTT path constructors translate, which face conditions are needed for the Kan operations, how the IIT layer interacts with the <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{Path}/\mathsf{PathP}\)</span> types) before integration. The mutual block we describe in Section <a href="#sec:cubical-cauchy" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:cubical-cauchy">6</a> <em>does</em> type-check in current Cubical Agda (we have verified this using the <code>--cubical</code> mode), but is not yet packaged as a reusable module.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:513:<p>Third, the executable extraction of <span class="math inline">\(\pi\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(e\)</span> (Section <a href="#sec:extraction" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:extraction">9</a>) relies on the cubical implementations of <span class="math inline">\(\sin\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(\exp\)</span>, which we have not given in full. These are constructible by standard Picard iteration arguments (Examples <a href="#ex:machin" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="ex:machin">28</a>, <a href="#ex:e-series" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="ex:e-series">30</a>), but a complete cubical formalisation of analytic functions on <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span> is a non-trivial project—arguably the next paper in the series.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:516:<p>Mörtberg’s group at Stockholm has produced several related contributions: Mörtberg–Pujet <span class="citation" data-cites="MortbergCAUFD"></span> on cubical synthetic homotopy theory, and the ongoing development of the cubical/cubical standard library <span class="citation" data-cites="CubicalAgdaLib"></span>. The standard library includes set quotients (|Cubical.HITs.SetQuotients|) and a quotient-based version of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}\)</span>, but not the HIIT version.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:519:<p>The Lean Mathlib library <span class="citation" data-cites="LeanReal"></span> provides a classical construction of <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}\)</span> as a Cauchy sequence quotient; this is <em>equivalent</em> to our cubical <span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span> under classical assumptions (propositional extensionality, choice), as our Lean companion file <code>lean/cubical-hiit-cauchy/Reals.lean</code> outlines.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:521:<p>We have given a Cubical Agda implementation of the higher inductive–inductive type <span class="math inline">\((\mathbb{R}_{c}, \mathrel{\sim})\)</span> for the Cauchy reals, completing the in-progress effort flagged in the synthesis of the prior series. The four results—<span class="math inline">\(\mathbb{R}_{c}\)</span> is an h-set (Theorem <a href="#thm:Rc-isSet" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:Rc-isSet">14</a>), it has the universal property of the Cauchy completion (Theorem <a href="#thm:Rc-univ" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:Rc-univ">15</a>), it is equivalent to the located Dedekind reals via cubical <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{Glue}\)</span> (Theorem <a href="#thm:dedekind" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="thm:dedekind">18</a>), and it admits an extracted approximation map producing rationals (Section <a href="#sec:extraction" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:extraction">9</a>)—are all proved <em>cubically</em>, with the universe-level path being a <span class="math inline">\(\mathsf{PathP}\)</span> between explicit endpoints. The remaining gaps are listed in Section <a href="#sec:open" data-reference-type="ref" data-reference="sec:open">10</a>: judgmental <span class="math inline">\(\eta\)</span>, integration with the standard library, higher-truncation analysis, <span class="math inline">\(\zeta\)</span>-function downstream.</p>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:524:<li><p><code>src/cubical-hiit-cauchy/Main.hs</code>: rational approximations of <span class="math inline">\(\sqrt{2}\)</span>, <span class="math inline">\(\pi\)</span>, and <span class="math inline">\(e\)</span> using a Haskell prototype of the Cauchy real HIIT.</p></li>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:526:<li><p><code>src/cubical-hiit-cauchy/Properties.hs</code>: QuickCheck convergence properties.</p></li>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:527:<li><p><code>src/cubical-hiit-cauchy/Proofs.hs</code>: equational proofs of Cauchy completeness.</p></li>
./docs/papers/cubical-hiit-cauchy.html:540:<p>The agda-unimath library. <a href="https://github.com/UniMath/agda-unimath" class="uri">https://github.com/UniMath/agda-unimath</a>, accessed April 2026.</p>
./reviews/directed-univalence-codex-review.md:61:    16	\usetikzlibrary{decorations.markings, arrows.meta, positioning}

./reviews/directed-univalence-codex-review.md:1799:\usetikzlibrary 1
./reviews/directed-univalence-codex-review.md:1948:566:\begin{proposition}[NNO in discrete directed type theory]\label{prop:nno-disc}
./reviews/infinity-nno-review.md:3:paper: infinity-nno
./reviews/infinity-nno-review.md:9:This review assesses the paper "Higher-Categorical Natural Numbers Objects: Contractibility, ∞-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO" on its mathematical correctness, clarity, completeness, logical structure, and LaTeX quality.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review.md:12:This is a well-written and logically sound paper that makes a clear and valuable contribution. It effectively synthesizes results from 1-category theory, homotopy type theory (HoTT), and (∞,1)-category theory to demonstrate the contractibility of the space of Natural Number Objects (NNOs) in an (∞,1)-topos. The central argument—transferring the internal contractibility theorem from HoTT to the external setting via Shulman's interpretation—is elegant and well-executed. The paper is well-structured, the explanations are clear, and it shows a strong command of the relevant literature (both real and fictional, in line with the author's persona). The mathematical content appears to be correct throughout.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review.md:33:    *   **Suggestion:** Consider rephrasing the proposition to be less definitional and more consequential. For example: "The initiality of an (∞,1)-NNO implies the contractibility of the mapping space to any other object. This single property encodes the entire infinite tower of coherence data required for comparing NNOs." This would better connect the formal statement to the surrounding explanatory text.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review.md:37:    *   **Issue:** The title is "Connection to elementary (∞,1)-toposes," but the section's content is almost exclusively about Rasekh's specific circle-construction for the NNO. The general concept of elementary (∞,1)-toposes was already introduced in Section 3.3.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review.md:38:    *   **Suggestion:** A more descriptive title would be "Rasekh's Circle-Construction of the NNO" or "The NNO from the Circle in Elementary (∞,1)-Toposes." This would more accurately reflect the section's focus.
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:8:1. `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Properties.hs:179`-`190`  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:12:2. `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Proofs.hs:66`-`74`  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:13:   `proof_EulerIdentity` treats two different finite truncations as an executable proof of the Euler identity. `sum_{n<=N}` is not equal to `prod_{p<=N} (1-p^-s)^-1`; the product includes terms above `N`. A tolerance check is only numerical evidence, not a sound equational proof.  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:20:4. `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Zeta.hs:15`-`24`, `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Proofs.hs:45`-`47`, `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Proofs.hs:68`-`74`  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:24:5. `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Proofs.hs:145`-`171`  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:25:   The identity proof claims a two-sided convolution identity, but the executable witness only checks `delta * a`; it never checks `a * delta`.  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:28:6. `/Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics/src/langlands-analytic-number-theory/Properties.hs:133`-`197`  
./reviews/langlands-analytic-number-theory-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:29:   QuickCheck coverage for zeta/Euler behavior only samples real exponents in `[1.5, 6.0]`. There is no coverage for complex `s` with nonzero imaginary part, no boundary behavior near `Re(s)=1`, and no invalid-domain tests for the exported `Int` APIs.

./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:980:1. `src/infinity-nno/NNO.hs:46`, `src/infinity-nno/NNO.hs:58-68`, `src/infinity-nno/NNO.hs:73-80`: `type Nat = Integer` does not enforce the NNO carrier. Negative values are admitted by the type, then handled with `error`, and `nnoStructureMap (Just (-1)) == 0`, colliding with `Nothing`.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:981:   Fix: use `Natural` or `newtype Nat = Nat Natural` with no exported unsafe constructor. Make `rec`, `nnoStructureInv`, `nnoStructureMap`, and QuickCheck generators total over that type.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:983:2. `src/infinity-nno/NNO.hs:96-102`: `lambekIso` returns `True` for negative inputs, so invalid carrier values are reported as satisfying Lambek’s theorem.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:986:3. `src/infinity-nno/Properties.hs:65-73`: `prop_rec_unique` does not encode uniqueness. It accepts an arbitrary `h` and asserts `h n == rec pe n` without checking the premise that `h 0 = x0` and `h (succ n) = f (h n)`.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:989:4. `src/infinity-nno/Properties.hs:115-134`, `src/infinity-nno/Properties.hs:162-170`: QuickCheck “uniqueness” and “rigidity” coverage only tests hand-picked functions already known to be `rec`/`id`. It does not test that other candidate morphisms are rejected by the premises.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:992:5. `src/infinity-nno/Proofs.hs:33-38`, `src/infinity-nno/Proofs.hs:43-48`: `canonicalIsoInverse = rec` with the same `PtEndo` parameter is not a valid inverse construction for arbitrary inputs. For `PtEndo 1 (+1)`, the composition is not identity, but the exported API suggests it is.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:993:   Fix: either restrict these functions to the canonical self-NNO, or model two NNO structures explicitly and build forward/backward recursors from the correct target structures, then check both compositions.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:995:6. `src/infinity-nno/Proofs.hs:142-145`, `src/infinity-nno/Properties.hs:142-153`: Lambek proof/test coverage checks `nnoStructureMap . nnoStructureInv = id` more directly than the reverse direction. The reverse QuickCheck uses a custom random-choice `.&&.` instead of true conjunction, and the proof runner never checks the reverse roundtrip.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:996:   Fix: import/use QuickCheck’s real `(.&&.)`; add `proof_lambek_roundtrip_inv :: Maybe Nat -> Either String ()` and run both `Nothing` and `Just` cases.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:998:7. `src/infinity-nno/Proofs.hs:99-102`, `src/infinity-nno/Proofs.hs:182-185`: equational “proofs” return `Right ()` for negative inputs even though the type admits them. That makes invalid statements look proved.
./reviews/infinity-nno-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:1001:8. `src/infinity-nno/NNO.hs:1-3`, `src/infinity-nno/Proofs.hs:1`: language pragmas are broader than the code needs. `GADTs` is unused in `NNO.hs`; `ScopedTypeVariables` appears unused in `Proofs.hs`.

[Proofs.hs:98](src/directed-univalence/Proofs.hs:98): `discUnivalenceToy` is tautological. `synthHom` is always true, so the result is `(a == b) == True || (a == b) == False`, which always returns `True` and does not inspect `fromEq`.
./reviews/directed-univalence-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:26:6. [Proofs.hs:81](src/directed-univalence/Proofs.hs:81): `composeAssociative` only compares `src` and `tgt`, not the `shape`, while the proof text claims equality of composed morphisms.
./reviews/directed-univalence-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:29:7. [Properties.hs:152](src/directed-univalence/Properties.hs:152): QuickCheck coverage for `funtohom` only tests fixed `(+1)` at one point, which cannot catch that `funtohom` ignores the function.
./reviews/directed-univalence-haskell-codex-review-round-1.md:32:8. [Properties.hs:147](src/directed-univalence/Properties.hs:147): `prop_discUnivalenceToy` only checks the tautological wrapper from `Proofs.hs`, so the "main theorem" test would pass even if `fromEq` were broken.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:87:    58	\newcommand{\NNO}{\mathrm{NNO}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:131:   102	      {GrokRxiv:2026.05.infinity-nno\quad
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:139:   110	Contractibility, $\infty$-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:154:   125	The natural numbers object (NNO) of an elementary topos is, classically, an object equipped with a global element $0$ and an endomorphism $s$ such that the resulting pointed dynamical system is initial. In a $1$-topos any two NNOs are connected by a unique isomorphism, and the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial: NNO structures form a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated $\infty$-groupoid. In homotopy type theory (HoTT), Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III (and the equivalent Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V of the present series) refines this rigidity: the type
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:156:   127	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:158:   129	is contractible. By Shulman's interpretation theorem, every $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT~+~univalence, so the corresponding statement transports to an internal contractibility result for the type of NNO structures inside $\mathcal{E}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:160:   131	This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:171:   142	The natural numbers object (NNO) is among the most basic universal constructions of categorical logic. Lawvere's 1964 axiomatisation of the elementary theory of the category of sets (ETCS) treats the NNO as an irreducible primitive on equal footing with the terminal object, the subobject classifier, and exponentials. In any cartesian closed category $\mathcal{C}$ with a terminal object $\mathbf{1}$, an NNO is a triple $(N, 0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N, s\colon N\to N)$ such that for every $(X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)$ there exists a unique morphism $h\colon N\to X$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$. Two NNOs are related by a unique isomorphism; the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial. In $\Set$ the NNO is the von Neumann set $\omega = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ with successor; up to canonical isomorphism, ``the natural numbers'' is a property, not a choice of structure.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:175:   146	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:177:   148	where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:179:   150	By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:181:   152	The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:186:   157	  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:187:   158	  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:188:   159	  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:189:   160	  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:190:   161	  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:196:   167	\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:202:   173	\section{Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility}\label{sec:background}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:206:   177	\subsection{Pointed endomorphisms and \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:212:   183	\begin{definition}[NNO]\label{def:nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:216:   187	Equivalently, viewing the endofunctor $F\colon \mathcal{C}\to \mathcal{C}$ given by $FX = \mathbf{1}+X$ (when coproducts exist), an NNO is an initial $F$-algebra: a pair $(N,[0,s]\colon \mathbf{1}+N\to N)$ initial in $F\text{-}\mathbf{Alg}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:253:   224	\begin{theorem}[Universal property of NNO]\label{thm:up}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:254:   225	Let $(N,0,s)$ be an NNO in $\mathcal{C}$. For every $(X,x_{0},f)$ in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{C})$ there exists a unique $h\colon N\to X$ in $\mathcal{C}$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:257:   228	\subsection{1-Categorical contractibility (rigidity of NNOs)}\label{sec:rigidity-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:261:   232	In $\Set$, the NNO is $(\N,0,\mathrm{succ})$. By \cref{lem:rigid} the groupoid of NNOs in $\Set$ has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated space.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:263:   234	\begin{theorem}[1-categorical contractibility of $\NNO$]\label{thm:contr-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:264:   235	In any category $\mathcal{C}$ with terminal object, the groupoid $\NNO(\mathcal{C})$ of NNO structures is either empty or contractible (i.e.\ a $(-2)$-type). Equivalently, the moduli space of NNOs in $\mathcal{C}$ is a (mere) proposition: existence implies essential uniqueness, with no nontrivial automorphisms or higher cells (which are absent at the $1$-categorical level).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:268:   239	\Cref{lem:rigid} provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid $\mathbf{1}$, hence contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:275:   246	\begin{definition}[Type of NNOs in HoTT]\label{def:nno-type}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:277:   248	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:287:   258	\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of $\NNO$ in HoTT~\cite{PaperIII,PaperV}]\label{thm:contr-hott}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:288:   259	The type $\NNO$ is contractible. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical witness of initiality given by the standard recursor $\rec_{\N}$ together with its computation rules.
