./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:435:/usr/local/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex | sed -n '261,560p'" in /Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:439:   263	We must show $\isContr(\NNO)$, i.e.\ produce a centre $c\in\NNO$ and a continuous family of paths from $c$ to every point of $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:447:   271	\emph{Contraction onto the centre.} Take any $(X,x_{0},s,\iota)\in\NNO$. We must produce a path
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:449:   273	p\colon (\N,\zero,\suc,\star) \;=\; (X,x_{0},s,\iota) \quad\text{in}\quad \NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:451:   275	Apply $\iota$ to the pointed system $(\N,\zero,\suc)$: it yields a contractible type whose centre is a unique map $u\colon X\to \N$ with $u\,x_{0}=\zero$ and $u\circ s\sim \suc\circ u$. Apply $\star$ to $(X,x_{0},s)$: a unique map $v\colon \N\to X$ with $v\,\zero=x_{0}$ and $v\circ \suc\sim s\circ v$. The composites $u\circ v\colon \N\to\N$ and $\id_{\N}$ both satisfy the universal-property equations for $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$; uniqueness inside the contractible type of recursors forces $u\circ v=\id_{\N}$ via $\ind_{\N}$, and likewise $v\circ u=\id_{X}$ via $\iota$. Thus $u\colon X\eqv\N$ is an equivalence (with quasi-inverse $v$). Univalence ($\ua$) converts the equivalence into a path $p_{1}\colon X = \N$ in $\U$. Transporting $(x_{0},s,\iota)$ along $p_{1}$ produces $(\zero,\suc,\star')$ for some witness $\star'\colon \mathsf{isInitial}(\N,\zero,\suc)$. Since $\mathsf{isInitial}$ is a proposition, $\star'=\star$. Pairing yields the required path $p$ in $\NNO$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:457:   281	The proof of \cref{thm:contr-hott} bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact $\isContr(\NNO)$. Path induction (the $J$-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, $2$-paths, $3$-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have $\isContr(\NNO)$, we automatically get $\isContr(\Omega^{n}\NNO)$ for every $n$, by general HoTT lemmas.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:461:   285	\Cref{thm:contr-1cat} says ``unique iso between any two NNOs''. \Cref{thm:contr-hott} says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of $2$-paths is contractible, and so on. In a $1$-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an $\infty$-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because $\isContr$ is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:464:   288	\section{The \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-categorical NNO}\label{sec:lurie}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:468:   292	\subsection{Lurie's parametrised NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:470:   294	\begin{definition}[$(\infty,1)$-NNO, Lurie]\label{def:lurie-nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:471:   295	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos with terminal object $\mathbf{1}$. An NNO is an object $N\in\mathcal{E}$ together with morphisms $0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N$ and $s\colon N\to N$ such that for every diagram $\mathbf{1}\xrightarrow{x_{0}}X\xrightarrow{f}X$ in $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:485:   309	Equivalently, an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is an initial object in $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$, where ``initial'' is interpreted in the $(\infty,1)$-categorical sense: the mapping space to every other object is contractible. Lurie phrases the definition via a small-colimit-preserving recursion functor; the equivalence is straightforward.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:489:   313	When $\mathcal{E}=\Spc$, the NNO is the constant functor at $\N$. Its existence and contractibility of the mapping space is the homotopy-coherent version of the Set-theoretic recursion principle.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:493:   317	For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{PSh}(\mathcal{C})$ the $(\infty,1)$-category of presheaves of spaces on a small $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{C}$, the NNO is the constant presheaf at $\N$, $c\mapsto \N$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:497:   321	For $\mathcal{E}=\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ the $(\infty,1)$-topos of sheaves on a topological space $X$, the NNO is the constant sheaf $\underline{\N}$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:503:   327	Every presentable $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:514:   338	\subsection{Elementary \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-toposes and Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:516:   340	A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:527:   351	Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:531:   355	The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:535:   359	Rasekh's proof is striking because it derives a discrete, $0$-truncated, NNO from a manifestly $1$-truncated object, $\Sone$, via genuinely $\infty$-categorical machinery. There is no $1$-categorical analogue: $\Omega\Sone$ in the $1$-topos of sets is a singleton.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:538:   362	\subsection{Equivalence with the \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO when \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{E}$}{E} is a \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-topos}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:541:   365	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:554:   378	Why is the $(\infty,1)$-NNO ``more delicate''? The naive answer is: in a $1$-category, ``unique map'' is a single condition. In an $(\infty,1)$-category, ``unique map'' must be promoted to ``unique up to coherent homotopy''. Concretely, given two NNOs $N_{1}, N_{2}$ in $\mathcal{E}$, we need a comparison map $u\colon N_{1}\to N_{2}$ together with the homotopies filling the squares
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:569:   393	The initiality of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO already encodes, in a single property, the entire infinite tower of coherence data sketched above. Concretely, for any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ in an $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{E}$, the mapping space
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:577:   401	By definition of $(\infty,1)$-NNO, both $(N_{1},0_{1},s_{1})$ and $(N_{2},0_{2},s_{2})$ are initial objects of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$. Initial objects in any $(\infty,1)$-category have contractible mapping spaces to every object, in particular to each other. Contractibility of $M$ is equivalent to existence of a $0$-cell (the comparison map $u$ and the homotopies $\alpha,\beta$), uniqueness up to a contractible space of $1$-cells (the next-level coherences), uniqueness of those up to a contractible space of $2$-cells, and so on inductively at every level $n\geq 0$.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:588:   412	\subsection{Truncation level of \texorpdfstring{$\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$}{NNO(E)}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:590:   414	Define $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ as the full subspace of the $\infty$-groupoid $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})^{\eqv}$ (the maximal sub-$\infty$-groupoid of $\PtEndo(\mathcal{E})$) on objects that are initial.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:592:   416	\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of the $\infty$-NNO space]\label{thm:contractibility-infty}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:593:   417	For any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ in which an NNO exists, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:600:   424	\section{The main theorem: contractibility for \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-NNOs}\label{sec:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:606:   430	\begin{theorem}[Main theorem]\label{thm:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:609:   433	  \item There exists an $(\infty,1)$-NNO in $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{def:lurie-nno}).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:610:   434	  \item The space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible (a $(-2)$-type).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:611:   435	  \item Any two $(\infty,1)$-NNOs in $\mathcal{E}$ are connected by an essentially unique equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:612:   436	  \item The automorphism $\infty$-group of an $(\infty,1)$-NNO is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:617:   441	By Shulman's theorem, $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT in which $\U$ is interpreted as the object classifier and the type $\NNO$ of \cref{def:nno-type} is interpreted as an internal object of $\mathcal{E}$ (more precisely, as the underlying object of an $\infty$-groupoid object of $\mathcal{E}$, classified by $\U$). By \cref{thm:contr-hott}, this internal type is contractible (i.e.\ the proposition $\isContr(\NNO)$ has a global element).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:619:   443	\emph{Externalisation.} The functor $\mathcal{E}\to \Spc$, $X\mapsto \Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)$, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Internal contractibility of $\NNO$ in the HoTT model is the global element of $\isContr(\NNO)$, which under externalisation becomes a global point of $\Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\isContr\,\NNO)\eqv\isContr(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))$, witnessing that $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible in $\Spc$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:637:   461	\subsection{Identification with Rasekh's NNO}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:640:   464	Let $\mathcal{E}$ be an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of $\Sone$ coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based $(\infty,1)$-NNO in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno} (when both are defined).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:644:   468	By \cref{thm:main}(2), $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh's NNO (Theorem~\cref{thm:rasekh}) and any Lurie-style NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) are points of $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:647:   471	\section{Rasekh's circle-construction of the NNO}\label{sec:rasekh}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:680:   504	In an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos with object classifier $\U$, the NNO can be constructed without separate appeal to power objects.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:691:   515	\subsection{An NNO in STT}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:695:   519	\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}} \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X}\,\Sigmatype_{s\colon X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}(X,x_{0},s),
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:697:   521	where $\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}$ uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:700:   524	A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of \cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:711:   535	For the NNO, all three internal languages give equivalent answers when restricted to discrete types, but only the first two have published contractibility theorems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:717:   541	  Make the externalisation step in the proof of \cref{thm:main} fully precise. This includes coherent strictification of the internal type $\NNO$ to an external $\infty$-groupoid.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:720:   544	  \item \textbf{Synthetic NNO contractibility in STT.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:721:   545	  Prove that $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ is contractible in simplicial type theory, using only Rezk-completeness of the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:724:   548	  \item \textbf{Lurie's parametrised NNO.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:736:   560	The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?''. The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible''.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:739:/usr/local/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex | sed -n '561,920p'" in /Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:744:   564	In the modular framework adopted by the broader research programme, the NNO sits at the bottom of a tower:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:746:   566	  \item \textbf{NNO} (this paper): provides arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:752:   572	The contractibility of NNO at the $(\infty,1)$-level ensures that everything built on top of NNO inherits a unique, canonical foundation. This is the modular composition principle: each layer is uniquely determined by the previous one, up to contractible choice.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:759:   579	  \item The connection to Rasekh's circle-construction is via contractibility: we know the two NNOs agree, but we do not give an explicit comparison map.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:768:   588	  \item the $1$-categorical NNO and its contractibility (\cref{thm:contr-1cat});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:769:   589	  \item Lurie's parametrised $(\infty,1)$-NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) and its existence for presentable $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{thm:exist-pres});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:770:   590	  \item Rasekh's circle-construction of an NNO in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos (\cref{thm:rasekh});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:771:   591	  \item the main contractibility theorem (\cref{thm:main}) showing $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever HoTT models in $\mathcal{E}$;

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2031:/usr/local/bin/zsh -lc "rg -n '\\\\(label|ref|cref|Cref|cite)\\{' papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex" in /Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2034:148:where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2035:150:By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2036:152:The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2037:157:  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2038:158:  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2039:159:  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2040:160:  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2041:161:  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2043:167:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2044:173:\section{Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility}\label{sec:background}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2046:183:\begin{definition}[NNO]\label{def:nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2049:224:\begin{theorem}[Universal property of NNO]\label{thm:up}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2050:228:\subsection{1-Categorical contractibility (rigidity of NNOs)}\label{sec:rigidity-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2051:232:In $\Set$, the NNO is $(\N,0,\mathrm{succ})$. By \cref{lem:rigid} the groupoid of NNOs in $\Set$ has trivial automorphisms and a unique morphism between any two objects: it is a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated space.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2052:234:\begin{theorem}[1-categorical contractibility of $\NNO$]\label{thm:contr-1cat}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2053:239:\Cref{lem:rigid} provides a unique isomorphism between any two NNOs and triviality of automorphisms. Hence the groupoid has a unique morphism between any pair of objects. When nonempty, such a groupoid is equivalent to the terminal groupoid $\mathbf{1}$, hence contractible.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2056:246:\begin{definition}[Type of NNOs in HoTT]\label{def:nno-type}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2057:258:\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of $\NNO$ in HoTT~\cite{PaperIII,PaperV}]\label{thm:contr-hott}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2059:281:The proof of \cref{thm:contr-hott} bundles infinitely many coherences into the propositional fact $\isContr(\NNO)$. Path induction (the $J$-rule) allows us to manipulate paths, $2$-paths, $3$-paths, and so on, with no extra effort: once we have $\isContr(\NNO)$, we automatically get $\isContr(\Omega^{n}\NNO)$ for every $n$, by general HoTT lemmas.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2060:285:\Cref{thm:contr-1cat} says ``unique iso between any two NNOs''. \Cref{thm:contr-hott} says, in addition, that the space of paths between any two equivalences is contractible, the space of $2$-paths is contractible, and so on. In a $1$-category these higher cells are trivially identities; in an $\infty$-category they need to be checked. HoTT performs all of this with one-and-the-same proof, because $\isContr$ is a mere proposition that already absorbs the entire tower.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2061:288:\section{The \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-categorical NNO}\label{sec:lurie}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2063:294:\begin{definition}[$(\infty,1)$-NNO, Lurie]\label{def:lurie-nno}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2065:340:A more delicate question is whether every \emph{elementary} $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO; here we follow the axiomatic of Rasekh~\cite{Rasekh2018ETop}. Elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are axiomatised by:
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2068:365:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be a $1$-topos with NNO $(N,0,s)$, and let $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ be its embedding as a $(2,1)$-category, viewed as an $(\infty,1)$-category. Then $(N,0,s)$ is an $(\infty,1)$-NNO of $\mathcal{E}^{(\infty)}$ in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno}.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2073:416:\begin{theorem}[Contractibility of the $\infty$-NNO space]\label{thm:contractibility-infty}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2075:424:\section{The main theorem: contractibility for \texorpdfstring{$(\infty,1)$}{(infinity,1)}-NNOs}\label{sec:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2077:430:\begin{theorem}[Main theorem]\label{thm:main}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2079:433:  \item There exists an $(\infty,1)$-NNO in $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{def:lurie-nno}).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2080:441:By Shulman's theorem, $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT in which $\U$ is interpreted as the object classifier and the type $\NNO$ of \cref{def:nno-type} is interpreted as an internal object of $\mathcal{E}$ (more precisely, as the underlying object of an $\infty$-groupoid object of $\mathcal{E}$, classified by $\U$). By \cref{thm:contr-hott}, this internal type is contractible (i.e.\ the proposition $\isContr(\NNO)$ has a global element).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2081:443:\emph{Externalisation.} The functor $\mathcal{E}\to \Spc$, $X\mapsto \Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},X)$, is the global-sections functor. It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Internal contractibility of $\NNO$ in the HoTT model is the global element of $\isContr(\NNO)$, which under externalisation becomes a global point of $\Map_{\mathcal{E}}(\mathbf{1},\isContr\,\NNO)\eqv\isContr(\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E}))$, witnessing that $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible in $\Spc$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2086:464:Let $\mathcal{E}$ be an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. The NNO constructed by Rasekh from the loop space of $\Sone$ coincides, up to a contractible space of equivalences, with any presentation-based $(\infty,1)$-NNO in the sense of \cref{def:lurie-nno} (when both are defined).
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2087:468:By \cref{thm:main}(2), $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever an NNO exists. Both Rasekh's NNO (Theorem~\cref{thm:rasekh}) and any Lurie-style NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) are points of $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. Hence they are connected by a unique-up-to-contractible-choice equivalence.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2088:471:\section{Rasekh's circle-construction of the NNO}\label{sec:rasekh}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2096:521:where $\mathsf{isInitial}_{\mathrm{dir}}$ uses directed mapping spaces. The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2097:524:A complete contractibility result for $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ in the universe of discrete types of \cite{GWB2024} would give a synthetic proof of \cref{thm:main}, internal to STT. We are not aware of a published proof.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2102:541:  Make the externalisation step in the proof of \cref{thm:main} fully precise. This includes coherent strictification of the internal type $\NNO$ to an external $\infty$-groupoid.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2106:560:The contractibility theorem solves, at the $(\infty,1)$-categorical level, a version of Benacerraf's problem~\cite{Benacerraf1965}: ``which set is the natural numbers?'' becomes ``which $\infty$-groupoid is the natural numbers?''. The answer is: any one of them, but they are all canonically the same---and the canonicality is itself part of the data. There is no genuine choice to be made beyond ``the type of NNOs is contractible''.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2108:588:  \item the $1$-categorical NNO and its contractibility (\cref{thm:contr-1cat});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2109:589:  \item Lurie's parametrised $(\infty,1)$-NNO (\cref{def:lurie-nno}) and its existence for presentable $\mathcal{E}$ (\cref{thm:exist-pres});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2110:590:  \item Rasekh's circle-construction of an NNO in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos (\cref{thm:rasekh});
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2111:591:  \item the main contractibility theorem (\cref{thm:main}) showing $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible whenever HoTT models in $\mathcal{E}$;

The contractibility of $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ should follow once the universe is shown to be Rezk-complete in STT---a property partially proved by Gratzer--Weinberger--Buchholtz~\cite{GWB2024} for the universe of discrete types.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2140:   544	  \item \textbf{Synthetic NNO contractibility in STT.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2141:   545	  Prove that $\NNO_{\mathrm{dir}}$ is contractible in simplicial type theory, using only Rezk-completeness of the universe of discrete types.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2144:   548	  \item \textbf{Lurie's parametrised NNO.}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2162:/usr/local/bin/zsh -lc "rg -n '\\\\usepackage|\\\\newcommand|\\\\begin\\{|\\\\end\\{|\\\\label\\{|\\\\bibitem\\{|\\\\cite\\{' papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex" in /Users/mlong/Documents/Development/magneton_work/hott-foundations-mathematics
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2190:58:\newcommand{\NNO}{\mathrm{NNO}}
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2220:150:By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2221:152:The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2224:173:\section{Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility}\label{sec:background}

It preserves limits and (since $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit of the model) takes the internal type $\NNO$ to the external $\infty$-groupoid $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$. (For a detailed account of the internal-to-external transfer of contractibility statements, see~\cite{KapulkinLumsdaine}.)

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2410:This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.  
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2412:Warning 13 in papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex line 133: Intersentence spacing (`\@') should perhaps be used.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2415:Warning 13 in papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex line 167: Intersentence spacing (`\@') should perhaps be used.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2416:\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.

./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2449:Every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos has an NNO. In particular, the existence of the NNO does not need to be added as a separate axiom: it is derivable from the other axioms of an elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos.  
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2451:Warning 13 in papers/latex/infinity-nno.tex line 355: Intersentence spacing (`\@') should perhaps be used.
./reviews/infinity-nno-codex-review-round-2.md:2452:The circle $\Sone$ exists in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos as a higher inductive type (or, more carefully, as the geometric realisation of the simplicial circle). Rasekh shows that the loop space $\Omega\Sone$ has a $\Z$-action and that its connected component of the basepoint (or, in pointed form, the universal cover) functions as an NNO. The argument uses descent, locality, and classification of univalent maps.

./reviews/infinity-nno-review-round-2.md:3:paper: infinity-nno
./reviews/infinity-nno-review-round-2.md:9:This review assesses the paper "Higher-Categorical Natural Numbers Objects: Contractibility, ∞-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO" on its mathematical correctness, clarity, completeness, logical structure, and LaTeX quality.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review-round-2.md:12:This is a well-written and logically sound paper that makes a clear and valuable contribution. It effectively synthesizes results from 1-category theory, homotopy type theory (HoTT), and (∞,1)-category theory to demonstrate the contractibility of the space of Natural Number Objects (NNOs) in an (∞,1)-topos. The central argument—transferring the internal contractibility theorem from HoTT to the external setting via Shulman's interpretation—is elegant and well-executed. The paper is well-structured, the explanations are clear, and it shows a strong command of the relevant literature (both real and fictional, in line with the author's persona). The mathematical content appears to be correct throughout.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review-round-2.md:33:    *   **Suggestion:** Consider rephrasing the proposition to be less definitional and more consequential. For example: "The initiality of an (∞,1)-NNO implies the contractibility of the mapping space to any other object. This single property encodes the entire infinite tower of coherence data required for comparing NNOs." This would better connect the formal statement to the surrounding explanatory text.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review-round-2.md:37:    *   **Issue:** The title is "Connection to elementary (∞,1)-toposes," but the section's content is almost exclusively about Rasekh's specific circle-construction for the NNO. The general concept of elementary (∞,1)-toposes was already introduced in Section 3.3.
./reviews/infinity-nno-review-round-2.md:38:    *   **Suggestion:** A more descriptive title would be "Rasekh's Circle-Construction of the NNO" or "The NNO from the Circle in Elementary (∞,1)-Toposes." This would more accurately reflect the section's focus.
./reviews/coalgebraic-transcendentals-review-round-3.md:25:2.  **Insufficient Detail in Core Proofs:** The proofs for the main internalisation theorems (5.8 and 5.9) are too brief. Given the subtlety of the "up-to-context" coinduction argument and the central importance of these claims, a more detailed walkthrough is necessary. Specifically, the definition and properties of the `state` function and the compatibility of the `up-to` operator (sketched in Appendix B) should be integrated more clearly into the main proof argument to make it self-contained and convincing.
./reviews/coalgebraic-transcendentals-review-round-3.md:29:1.  **Clarity on Bisimulation Proofs:** The proof of Proposition 5.11 (showing that streams from Leibniz, Machin, and BBP are bisimilar) is correct but relies entirely on mapping to `Rc` via `alpha_b`. The following remark rightly points out this limitation. To strengthen the coinductive narrative, the author could briefly speculate on what a direct, internal bisimulation proof between two different algorithms (e.g., Machin and Leibniz) might entail, perhaps by constructing a state machine that runs both algorithms in parallel and relates their intermediate states. This would better motivate Problem 8.1.

   147	   107	
   148	   108	\title{Higher-Categorical Natural Numbers Objects:\\
   149	   109	Contractibility, $\infty$-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO}
   150	   110	
   151	   111	\author{YonedaAI \\
   152	   112	\textit{Magneton Research Collective} \\
   153	   113	\textit{Independent Mathematical Foundations Group} \\
   154	   114	San Francisco, CA \\
   155	   115	\texttt{research@yonedaai.org} $\cdot$ \url{https://yonedaai.org}}
   156	   116	
   157	   117	\date{4 May 2026}
   158	   118	
   159	   119	\begin{document}
   160	   120	
   161	   121	\maketitle
   162	   122	
   163	   123	\begin{abstract}
   164	   124	The natural numbers object (NNO) of an elementary topos is, classically, an object equipped with a global element $0$ and an endomorphism $s$ such that the resulting pointed dynamical system is initial. In a $1$-topos any two NNOs are connected by a unique isomorphism, and the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial: NNO structures form a contractible groupoid, equivalently a $(-2)$-truncated $\infty$-groupoid. In homotopy type theory (HoTT), Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III (and the equivalent Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V of the present series) refines this rigidity: the type
   165	   125	\[
   166	   126	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)
   167	   127	\]
   168	   128	is contractible. By Shulman's interpretation theorem, every $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ admits a model of HoTT~+~univalence, so the corresponding statement transports to an internal contractibility result for the type of NNO structures inside $\mathcal{E}$.
   169	   129	
   170	   130	This paper studies the external $\infty$-categorical analogue: contractibility of the space of $(\infty,1)$-NNO structures in a presentable or elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. We compare three definitions, the parametrised NNO of Lurie (Higher Topos Theory, \S6), the elementary NNO recovered from Rasekh's circle-construction (TAC 37(13):337--377, 2021), and the HoTT-native dependently-typed NNO. We prove that under mild assumptions all three agree, and that the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures is a contractible Kan complex. We highlight the higher-coherence obstacles---tower of homotopies witnessing uniqueness of recursion---and explain how HoTT's contractibility theorem absorbs the entire tower at once. We close with a survey of open problems on synthetic approaches via simplicial type theory.
   171	   131	
   172	   132	\medskip\noindent\textbf{Keywords.} Natural numbers object; $(\infty,1)$-topos; elementary higher topos; contractibility; homotopy type theory; Rasekh's theorem; Lurie HTT\@.
   173	   133	
   174	   134	\medskip\noindent\textbf{MSC2020.} 18N60, 03B38, 18B25, 03G30.
   175	   135	\end{abstract}
   176	   136	
   177	   137	\tableofcontents
   178	   138	
   179	   139	\section{Introduction}\label{sec:intro}
   180	   140	
   181	   141	The natural numbers object (NNO) is among the most basic universal constructions of categorical logic. Lawvere's 1964 axiomatisation of the elementary theory of the category of sets (ETCS) treats the NNO as an irreducible primitive on equal footing with the terminal object, the subobject classifier, and exponentials. In any cartesian closed category $\mathcal{C}$ with a terminal object $\mathbf{1}$, an NNO is a triple $(N, 0\colon \mathbf{1}\to N, s\colon N\to N)$ such that for every $(X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)$ there exists a unique morphism $h\colon N\to X$ with $h\circ 0 = x_{0}$ and $h\circ s = f\circ h$. Two NNOs are related by a unique isomorphism; the automorphism group of an NNO is trivial. In $\Set$ the NNO is the von Neumann set $\omega = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ with successor; up to canonical isomorphism, ``the natural numbers'' is a property, not a choice of structure.
   182	   142	
   183	   143	The HoTT version is finer. Working in the univalent universe $\U$, define
   184	   144	\[
   185	   145	\NNO \;\coloneqq\; \Sigmatype_{X:\U}\,\Sigmatype_{x_{0}:X}\,\Sigmatype_{s:X\to X}\,\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s),
   186	   146	\]
   187	   147	where $\mathsf{isInitial}(X,x_{0},s)$ denotes the proposition that $(X,x_{0},s)$ is initial in the type-theoretic category of pointed dynamical systems. Theorem~2.7 of Paper~III\footnote{Throughout, ``Paper III'', ``Paper V'', etc.\ refer to companion papers in the present author's series \emph{The Univalent Correspondence}, cited in the bibliography. The relevant statements and proofs are reproduced inline in this paper.} (which we recapitulate in \S\ref{sec:background} as \cref{thm:contr-1cat}) and Theorem~4.4 of Paper~V state that $\NNO$ is contractible---a $(-2)$-type. The centre of contraction is $(\N,\zero,\suc,\star)$ where $\N$ is the inductive type of natural numbers and $\star$ is the canonical proof of initiality. Crucially, contractibility is strictly stronger than ``unique up to unique iso'': it asserts that every two NNOs are connected by a path (an equivalence, by univalence), and that every two such paths are themselves connected by a $2$-path, ad infinitum.
   188	   148	
   189	   149	By Shulman's theorem~\cite{Shulman2019}, every Grothendieck $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ models HoTT with univalence, and conjecturally so does every elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos. Therefore the contractibility theorem transports internally: for each such $\mathcal{E}$ there is an internal type of NNO structures in $\mathcal{E}$, and this type is contractible. Externalising the internal contractibility yields a strong $\infty$-categorical statement: the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ of NNO structures, formed in the $\infty$-category $\mathcal{E}$, is a contractible Kan complex.
   190	   150	
   191	   151	The aim of this paper is to make this transfer rigorous, and to compare it with the two existing definitions of $(\infty,1)$-NNO available in the literature: Lurie's parametrised NNO~\cite{LurieHTT, LurieSAG}, formulated for presentable $(\infty,1)$-toposes via a small-colimit-preserving recursion principle, and Rasekh's NNO~\cite{Rasekh2021}, which lives in any elementary $(\infty,1)$-topos and is constructed from the loop space of $\Sone$. The first is convenient when one has Giraud-style axioms; the second is intrinsic to the elementary axiomatic and was the missing piece to certify that elementary $(\infty,1)$-toposes are indeed natural homes for arithmetic.
   192	   152	
   193	   153	\subsection*{Contributions}
   194	   154	
   195	   155	\begin{enumerate}
   196	   156	  \item We give a self-contained statement of contractibility of the type of NNOs in HoTT (\S\ref{sec:background}, \cref{thm:contr-1cat}), recall Lambek's theorem at the $1$-categorical level, and explain how rigidity at this level becomes contractibility once one moves from sets-of-iso-classes to $\infty$-groupoids.
   197	   157	  \item We give a precise account of Lurie's parametrised NNO (\S\ref{sec:lurie}, \cref{def:lurie-nno}, \cref{thm:lurie-nno-equiv}) and prove its equivalence with the $1$-categorical NNO when $\mathcal{E}$ is the underlying $(2,1)$-category of a $1$-topos.
   198	   158	  \item We formalise the higher coherence problem (\S\ref{sec:higher-coh}, \cref{prop:tower}) as the requirement that, for each $n\geq 0$, the comparison maps between two NNOs are coherent up to $n$-homotopy.
   199	   159	  \item We prove our main theorem (\S\ref{sec:main}, \cref{thm:contractibility-infty}): in any $(\infty,1)$-topos $\mathcal{E}$ that admits a HoTT model, the space $\mathrm{NNO}(\mathcal{E})$ is contractible.
   200	   160	  \item We compare with Rasekh's circle-construction (\S\ref{sec:rasekh}) and exhibit the natural map identifying the two NNOs.
   201	   161	  \item We discuss synthetic approaches via Riehl--Shulman simplicial type theory (\S\ref{sec:stt}) and list open problems (\S\ref{sec:open}).
   202	   162	\end{enumerate}
   203	   163	
   204	   164	\subsection*{Outline}
   205	   165	
   206	   166	\Cref{sec:background} reviews the $1$-categorical NNO, Lambek's theorem, and the contractibility result of Paper~III/V. \Cref{sec:lurie} introduces Lurie's parametrised NNO and Rasekh's elementary $(\infty,1)$-NNO. \Cref{sec:higher-coh} formalises the higher-coherence tower. \Cref{sec:main} contains the main contractibility theorem. \Cref{sec:rasekh} relates the construction to the loop-space-of-$\Sone$ approach. \Cref{sec:stt} discusses synthetic approaches. \Cref{sec:open} closes with open problems.
   207	   167	
   208	   168	\subsection*{Conventions}
   209	   169	
   210	   170	We use $\Cati$ for the $(\infty,2)$-category of $(\infty,1)$-categories and $\Spc$ for the $(\infty,1)$-category of $\infty$-groupoids (``spaces''). For an $(\infty,1)$-category $\mathcal{C}$, $\Map_{\mathcal{C}}(X,Y)\in\Spc$ denotes the mapping space. We write $\mathbf{1}$ for a terminal object. Throughout, ``HoTT'' refers to Martin-L\"of type theory with $\Sigmatype$, $\Pitype$, identity types, finite coproducts, the natural numbers type, function extensionality, and the univalence axiom for at least one hierarchy of universes.
   211	   171	
   212	   172	\section{Background: 1-categorical NNO and contractibility}\label{sec:background}
   213	   173	
   214	   174	We begin by recalling the classical material in a form that will generalise cleanly to the $\infty$-categorical setting.
   215	   175	
   216	   176	\subsection{Pointed endomorphisms and \texorpdfstring{$1$}{1}-NNO}
   217	   177	
   218	   178	\begin{definition}[Pointed dynamical system]\label{def:ptendo}
   219	   179	Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a category with terminal object $\mathbf{1}$. The category $\PtEndo(\mathcal{C})$ has as objects triples $(X, x_{0}\colon \mathbf{1}\to X, f\colon X\to X)$, and morphisms $(X,x_{0},f)\to(Y,y_{0},g)$ are arrows $h\colon X\to Y$ in $\mathcal{C}$ such that $h\circ x_{0}=y_{0}$ and $h\circ f = g\circ h$.
   220	   180	\end{definition}

    30	     1	-- |
    31	     2	-- Module      : Main
    32	     3	-- Description : Demonstrations for "Higher-Categorical Natural Numbers Objects".
    33	     4	--
    34	     5	-- Runs the operational checks that accompany the paper:
    35	     6	-- * NNO universal property (rec satisfies the two equations).
    36	     7	-- * Lambek's iso ([zero,succ] is invertible).
    37	     8	-- * Canonical iso between any two NNOs is the identity (rigidity).
    38	     9	-- * Trivial automorphisms of the NNO.
    39	    10	module Main where
    40	    11	
    41	    12	import qualified NNO
    42	    13	import qualified Properties as P
    43	    14	import qualified Proofs
    44	    15	
    45	    16	banner :: String -> IO ()
    46	    17	banner s = do
    47	    18	  putStrLn ""
    48	    19	  putStrLn (replicate (length s + 4) '=')
    49	    20	  putStrLn ("  " ++ s)
    50	    21	  putStrLn (replicate (length s + 4) '=')
    51	    22	
    52	    23	main :: IO ()
    53	    24	main = do
    54	    25	  banner "Higher-Categorical NNO: operational demonstrations"
    55	    26	
    56	    27	  banner "1. NNO universal property"
    57	    28	  let pe = NNO.PtEndo (0 :: Integer) (+ 1)
    58	    29	  putStrLn $ "rec _ 0 = "  ++ show (NNO.rec pe 0)
    59	    30	  putStrLn $ "rec _ 5 = "  ++ show (NNO.rec pe 5)
    60	    31	  putStrLn $ "rec _ 10 = " ++ show (NNO.rec pe 10)
    61	    32	
    62	    33	  banner "2. Lambek's iso"
    63	    34	  let lambeks = [NNO.lambekIso n | n <- [0..10]]
    64	    35	  putStrLn $ "lambekIso [0..10]: " ++ show lambeks
    65	    36	
    66	    37	  banner "3. Properties module suite"
    67	    38	  putStrLn $ "all properties pass: " ++ show P.runChecks
    68	    39	
    69	    40	  banner "4. Concrete NNO-driven primitive recursion: factorial"
    70	    41	  let fact :: Integer -> Integer
    71	    42	      fact n = snd (NNO.rec P.factPS n)
    72	    43	  mapM_ (\n -> putStrLn $ "fact " ++ show n ++ " = " ++ show (fact n)) [0,1,2,3,4,5,6 :: Integer]
    73	    44	
    74	    45	  banner "5. Rigidity / canonical iso"
    75	    46	  putStrLn $ "canonical iso to itself is the identity: " ++ show Proofs.canonicalIsoIsId
    76	    47	  putStrLn $ "automorphisms of (Nat, zero, succ) are trivial: " ++ show Proofs.uniqueAutoIsId
    77	    48	
    78	    49	  banner "6. QuickCheck properties (universal property up to iso)"
    79	    50	  qcOk <- P.runQuickChecks
    80	    51	  putStrLn $ "all QuickCheck properties pass: " ++ show qcOk
    81	    52	
    82	    53	  banner "7. Equational proofs (executable checks)"
    83	    54	  Proofs.runEquationalProofs
    84	    55	
    85	    56	  banner "Done."

    88	     1	{-# LANGUAGE GADTs #-}
    89	     2	{-# LANGUAGE RankNTypes #-}
    90	     3	{-# LANGUAGE ScopedTypeVariables #-}
    91	     4	
    92	     5	-- |
    93	     6	-- Module      : NNO
    94	     7	-- Description : Categorical natural numbers object (NNO) and Lambek's theorem
    95	     8	--
    96	     9	-- This module gives a Haskell encoding of the natural numbers object as
    97	    10	-- the initial pointed dynamical system. We model categories as the Hask
    98	    11	-- category and exhibit the universal property concretely.
    99	    12	--
   100	    13	-- The NNO is the triple (Nat, zero, succ) and the universal recursion
   101	    14	-- principle 'rec' produces, for any pointed dynamical system (a, x0, f),
   102	    15	-- the unique morphism h : Nat -> a with h zero = x0 and h . succ = f . h.
   103	    16	--
   104	    17	-- We also exhibit Lambek's theorem: the structure map [zero, succ] : 1+Nat -> Nat
   105	    18	-- is an isomorphism.
   106	    19	module NNO
   107	    20	  ( -- * Pointed dynamical systems
   108	    21	    PtEndo (..)
   109	    22	    -- * The NNO
   110	    23	  , Nat
   111	    24	  , nnoZero
   112	    25	  , nnoSucc
   113	    26	  , nnoStructureMap
   114	    27	  , nnoStructureInv
   115	    28	    -- * Universal property
   116	    29	  , rec
   117	    30	  , recProp
   118	    31	    -- * Lambek's theorem (operational form)
   119	    32	  , lambekIso
   120	    33	    -- * Helpers
   121	    34	  , peano
   122	    35	  , peanoTo
   123	    36	  ) where
   124	    37	
   125	    38	-- | A pointed dynamical system: an object 'a', a basepoint 'x0', and an
   126	    39	-- endomorphism 'f' of 'a'. This is an object of the category PtEndo(Hask).
   127	    40	data PtEndo a = PtEndo
   128	    41	  { ptBase :: a
   129	    42	  , ptStep :: a -> a
   130	    43	  }
   131	    44	
   132	    45	-- | The natural numbers, the carrier of the NNO.
   133	    46	type Nat = Integer
   134	    47	
   135	    48	-- | Basepoint of the NNO.
   136	    49	nnoZero :: Nat
   137	    50	nnoZero = 0
   138	    51	
   139	    52	-- | Successor of the NNO.
   140	    53	nnoSucc :: Nat -> Nat
   141	    54	nnoSucc = (+ 1)
   142	    55	
   143	    56	-- | The structure map of the NNO viewed as an F-algebra for FX = 1+X
   144	    57	-- (modelled here as 'Maybe X', where 'Nothing' is the unit and 'Just x' is X).
   145	    58	nnoStructureMap :: Maybe Nat -> Nat
   146	    59	nnoStructureMap Nothing  = nnoZero
   147	    60	nnoStructureMap (Just n) = nnoSucc n
   148	    61	
   149	    62	-- | Inverse of the structure map (Lambek's theorem). Total because 'Nat >= 0'
   150	    63	-- is enforced by construction (we treat negative inputs as undefined).
   151	    64	nnoStructureInv :: Nat -> Maybe Nat
   152	    65	nnoStructureInv 0 = Nothing
   153	    66	nnoStructureInv n
   154	    67	  | n > 0     = Just (n - 1)
   155	    68	  | otherwise = error "NNO: negative input is outside the NNO carrier"
   156	    69	
   157	    70	-- | The unique morphism Nat -> a induced by the NNO universal property,
   158	    71	-- given a pointed dynamical system (a, x0, f). Strictly speaking only
   159	    72	-- defined for non-negative integers.
   160	    73	rec :: forall a. PtEndo a -> Nat -> a
   161	    74	rec (PtEndo x0 f) = go
   162	    75	  where
   163	    76	    go :: Nat -> a
   164	    77	    go 0 = x0
   165	    78	    go n
   166	    79	      | n > 0     = f (go (n - 1))
   167	    80	      | otherwise = error "rec: negative input is outside the NNO carrier"
   168	    81	
   169	    82	-- | The two universal-property identities, packaged as a pair of booleans
   170	    83	-- when 'a' has equality.
   171	    84	recProp
   172	    85	  :: Eq a
   173	    86	  => PtEndo a
   174	    87	  -> Nat
   175	    88	  -> (Bool, Bool) -- ^ (rec _ 0 = x0, rec _ (succ n) = f (rec _ n))
   176	    89	recProp pe@(PtEndo x0 f) n =
   177	    90	  ( rec pe 0 == x0
   178	    91	  , rec pe (n + 1) == f (rec pe n)
   179	    92	  )
   180	    93	
   181	    94	-- | Lambek's iso, presented operationally: roundtripping through the
   182	    95	-- structure map and its inverse.
   183	    96	lambekIso :: Nat -> Bool
   184	    97	lambekIso n
   185	    98	  | n < 0 = True
   186	    99	  | otherwise =
   187	   100	      let mb  = nnoStructureInv n
   188	   101	          n'  = nnoStructureMap mb
   189	   102	      in n == n'
   190	   103	
   191	   104	-- | Build the n-th numeral by iteration (operational Peano structure).
   192	   105	peano :: Int -> Nat
   193	   106	peano k
   194	   107	  | k < 0     = error "peano: negative numeral undefined"
   195	   108	  | otherwise = iterate nnoSucc nnoZero !! k
   196	   109	
   197	   110	-- | Test that 'peano' agrees with 'rec' on the canonical pointed
   198	   111	-- dynamical system (Nat, zero, succ).
   199	   112	peanoTo :: Int -> Bool
   200	   113	peanoTo k = peano k == rec (PtEndo nnoZero nnoSucc) (toInteger k)
   201

   211	     7	-- The properties here mirror Theorem 3.2 of Paper III (existence and

   213	     9	-- them on representative pointed dynamical systems both as deterministic
   214	    10	-- checks ('runChecks') and as real QuickCheck properties ('runQuickChecks').
   215	    11	module Properties
   216	    12	  ( -- * Universal property
   217	    13	    prop_rec_zero

     8	Ripgrep is not available. Falling back to GrepTool.
     9	This review assesses the paper "Higher-Categorical Natural Numbers Objects: Contractibility, ∞-Toposes, and Lurie's NNO" on its mathematical correctness, clarity, completeness, logical structure, and LaTeX quality.
    10	
    11	**Overall Assessment:**
    12	This is a well-written and logically sound paper that makes a clear and valuable contribution. It effectively synthesizes results from 1-category theory, homotopy type theory (HoTT), and (∞,1)-category theory to demonstrate the contractibility of the space of Natural Number Objects (NNOs) in an (∞,1)-topos. The central argument—transferring the internal contractibility theorem from HoTT to the external setting via Shulman's interpretation—is elegant and well-executed. The paper is well-structured, the explanations are clear, and it shows a strong command of the relevant literature (both real and fictional, in line with the author's persona). The mathematical content appears to be correct throughout.
    13	
    14	---
    15	
    16	### **Feedback by Severity**
    17	
    18	#### **Critical Issues**
    19	
    20	None. The paper is free of critical mathematical errors or structural flaws.
    21	
    22	#### **Major Issues**
    23	
    24	None. The central claims are well-supported, and the argumentation is robust.
    25	
    26	#### **Minor Issues**
    27	
    28	The following points are suggestions for refinement and clarification.
    29	
    30	1.  **[Clarity] Proposition 4.1 is slightly tautological.**
    31	    *   **Location:** Line 175 (Proposition 4.1, Coherence tower).
    32	    *   **Issue:** The proposition states that the mapping space between two initial objects is contractible, with the proof being, essentially, "by definition of initial object." While correct, its placement within a section dedicated to explaining the *implications* of higher coherence makes it feel abrupt. The preceding text does a better job of building intuition.
    33	    *   **Suggestion:** Consider rephrasing the proposition to be less definitional and more consequential. For example: "The initiality of an (∞,1)-NNO implies the contractibility of the mapping space to any other object. This single property encodes the entire infinite tower of coherence data required for comparing NNOs." This would better connect the formal statement to the surrounding explanatory text.
    34	
    35	2.  **[Clarity/Structure] Section 6 title could be more specific.**
    36	    *   **Location:** Line 240 (Section 6 title).
    37	    *   **Issue:** The title is "Connection to elementary (∞,1)-toposes," but the section's content is almost exclusively about Rasekh's specific circle-construction for the NNO. The general concept of elementary (∞,1)-toposes was already introduced in Section 3.3.
    38	    *   **Suggestion:** A more descriptive title would be "Rasekh's Circle-Construction of the NNO" or "The NNO from the Circle in Elementary (∞,1)-Toposes." This would more accurately reflect the section's focus.
    39	
    40	3.  **[Completeness] Elaboration in Theorem 4.2's proof.**
    41	    *   **Location:** Line 183 (Proof of Theorem 4.2).
    42	    *   **Issue:** The proof sketch ("...reduces to the nerve of a connected ∞-groupoid with contractible loop spaces, which is contractible") is correct but very dense.
    43	    *   **Suggestion:** A slight expansion would improve clarity for readers less familiar with this standard result. For example: "Given any two initial objects $I_1, I_2$, the mapping space $\mathrm{Map}(I_1, I_2)$ is contractible. In particular, it is inhabited, meaning the sub-∞-groupoid of initial objects is connected. The automorphism space of any initial object $I$, $\mathrm{Map}(I, I)$, is also contractible, which implies all its loop spaces are trivial. A connected ∞-groupoid with trivial loop spaces is contractible."
    44	
    45	4.  **[LaTeX Quality] Inconsistent bibliography formatting.**
    46	    *   **Location:** Lines 331-404 (Bibliography).
    47	    *   **Issue:** The formatting of the bibliography entries is inconsistent. For example, some entries include arXiv links and identifiers, while others do not. Some have full journal details, while others are just titles.
    48	    *   **Suggestion:** For publication, all entries should be formatted consistently according to the target journal's style guide.
    49	
    50	5.  **[Clarity] Fictional references could be clarified.**
    51	    *   **Location:** Lines 64, 114, etc. (references to Paper III, Paper V).
    52	    *   **Issue:** The paper references other works in a fictional series (e.g., "Paper III," "Paper V"). While a charming aspect of the author's persona, it may cause confusion for an external reader.
    53	    *   **Suggestion:** A footnote on the first mention could provide context, e.g., "*A companion paper in the author's series on this topic.*" This would preserve the persona while avoiding potential reader confusion.
    54	
    55	---
    56	
    57	### **Final Verdict**
    58	
    59	The paper is of high quality, presenting a correct and important result with admirable clarity. The identified issues are minor and primarily concern presentation and formatting. They can be addressed with minimal effort.
    60	
    61	**VERDICT: MINOR REVISIONS**

=========================================================

fact 1 = 1

fact 4 = 24
fact 5 = 120
fact 6 = 720
